docs.title: About the SCPS Tech Hub
docs.text: The SCPS Tech Hub is a collection of step-by-step instructional technology guides built specifically for Shenandoah County Public Schools teachers and staff.

docs.title: Who maintains this?
docs.text: This site is maintained by the SCPS Instructional Technology Resource Teachers (ITRTs). Guides are written with SCPS-specific settings, workflows, and best practices in mind.

docs.title: Why does this exist?
docs.text: Generic help documentation doesn't account for how we use our tools at SCPS. These guides are tailored to our division's configuration, our students, and our instructional priorities.

docs.title: Have feedback?
docs.text: See the Contributing page to learn how to suggest new guides or report issues.

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: Want to suggest a new guide, report an error, or contribute content? Here's how.

docs.title: Suggest a new guide
docs.text:
If there's a tool or task you'd like a guide for, reach out to your building ITRT with:

  • The tool name and specific task you need help with
  • Any screenshots of where you're getting stuck
  • Whether this is something your whole team could benefit from

docs.title: Report an error
docs.text:
If you find incorrect information in a guide:

  • Note the guide title and step number where the error is
  • Describe what's wrong and what the correct information should be
  • Send it to your building ITRT

docs.title: Contribute a guide
docs.text:
If you're an ITRT or tech-savvy teacher who wants to write a guide:

  1. Guides are written in Markdown — a simple text formatting language
  2. Follow the existing guide templates for structure and tone
  3. Submit your draft to the Tech Hub maintainer for review

Guide style tips

  • Write for a teacher who has never used the tool before
  • Use numbered steps for procedures
  • Include tips, warnings, and notes using callout boxes
  • Add screenshot placeholders where visuals would help
  • End every guide with a "What's next?" section linking to related guides

docs.title: Quick Start Guides
docs.text:
Get up and running with any tool in under 5 minutes. These are the essentials — just enough to get started.

Quick start guides are coming soon for each tool. In the meantime, check the full tutorials under Browse by Tool.

docs.title: Canvas LMS
docs.text: Canvas is the learning management system (LMS) used across SCPS for course management, assignments, grading, communication, and more. It's the central hub where students access their coursework and where you manage your classroom digitally.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
New to Canvas or need a refresher?

Guide Time Description Navigating the Canvas dashboard ~5 min Understand the dashboard, course navigation, and global menu Setting up your course homepage ~8 min Choose a layout and customize what students see first Organizing content with modules ~10 min Build a clear structure students can follow

docs.title: Assignments & Assessments
docs.text:
Create, distribute, and collect student work.

Guide Time Description Creating an assignment ~8 min Build assignments with submission types, due dates, and points Building a quiz with New Quizzes ~10 min Create auto-graded assessments with question banks Creating a graded discussion ~5 min Use discussions as a graded participation tool Using rubrics ~8 min Create and attach rubrics for consistent grading

docs.title: Grading & Feedback
docs.text:
Grade efficiently and give meaningful feedback.

Guide Time Description Using SpeedGrader ~8 min Grade submissions, annotate documents, and leave feedback Managing the Gradebook ~10 min Organize grades, set up weighting, and post scores

docs.title: Communication
docs.text:
Stay connected with students and parents.

Guide Time Description Announcements and Inbox ~5 min Send class-wide announcements and individual messages

docs.title: Tips & Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Guide Time Description Canvas tips and best practices ~5 min Power user strategies for K-12 teachers

SCPS-Specific Notes

  • Canvas courses are automatically created from your SIS roster. You do not need to create courses manually.
  • Your Canvas account links to MasteryConnect for standards-based assessment data. See the MasteryConnect guides for details.
  • Students access Canvas at your division's Canvas URL. The Canvas Student app is also available on Chromebooks and mobile devices.
  • Parent/guardian access is available through the Canvas Parent app — encourage families to set this up.

docs.title: Announcements and Inbox
docs.text:
Canvas gives you two main communication tools: Announcements for broadcasting to your class, and the Inbox for direct messaging. This guide covers both.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Announcements
docs.text: Announcements are class-wide messages that appear at the top of your course and send notifications to students.

docs.title: Creating an announcement
docs.text:
1. Go to Announcements in the course navigation.
  2. Click "+ Announcement".
  3. Add a title and write your message using the Rich Content Editor.
  4. Optionally:
    • Attach a file or embed media
    • Delay posting — schedule it for a future date and time
    • Allow users to comment — let students respond to the announcement
  5. Click "Save" to post immediately (or it will post at your scheduled time).

docs.title: When to use announcements
docs.text:
Scenario Example Weekly overview "This week we're working on Unit 4. Here's what to expect..." Due date reminder "Reminder: Your essay is due Friday at 11:59 PM." Class update "Great job on yesterday's discussion! Here are some highlights..." Resource sharing "I found this video that explains the concept really well — check it out."

Post weekly announcements

A short Monday morning announcement outlining the week's plan keeps students on track and reduces "what are we doing?" questions. It takes 2 minutes to write and saves you countless interruptions.

docs.title: Canvas Inbox
docs.text: The Inbox is Canvas's internal messaging system. It works like email but stays within Canvas.

docs.title: Sending a message
docs.text:
1. Click the Inbox icon in the global navigation (left sidebar).
  2. Click the compose icon (pencil/paper icon).
  3. Select the course from the dropdown.
  4. Add recipients:
    • Type a student's name to search
    • Select "All in [Course Name]" to message the whole class
    • Select a specific section or group
  5. Add a subject and write your message.
  6. Click "Send".

docs.title: Sending messages from the Gradebook
docs.text:
One of the most powerful Inbox features is messaging students based on grade criteria:

  1. In the Gradebook, click the three dots on an assignment column.
  2. Select "Message Students Who..."
  3. Choose a criteria:
    • Haven't submitted yet
    • Haven't been graded
    • Scored more than [X]
    • Scored less than [X]
  4. Canvas auto-populates the recipient list. Write your message and send.

Targeted messages save time

Instead of sending a blanket "turn in your work" message to everyone, use "Message Students Who haven't submitted yet." Students who already turned in work won't get an unnecessary reminder.

docs.title: Communication best practices
docs.text:
Tips from your ITRT

  • Use Announcements for the whole class, Inbox for individual or small group messages.
  • Keep Announcements short — students skim. Put the most important info in the first sentence.
  • Check your Inbox regularly — students may message you with questions, especially outside of class.
  • Encourage parents to use the Canvas Parent app — it gives families visibility into announcements, grades, and upcoming assignments without needing to message you.
  • Don't use Inbox for urgent issues — Canvas Inbox is not real-time. For urgent matters, use email or phone.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Managing the Gradebook — Message students based on grade data
  • Canvas tips and best practices — More strategies for effective Canvas use

docs.title: Setting Up Your Course Homepage
docs.text:
The homepage is the first thing students see when they enter your course. A well-designed homepage reduces confusion and helps students find what they need immediately.

Time to complete: ~8 minutes

docs.title: Choosing a homepage layout
docs.text:
Canvas offers several homepage options. Go to your course Home page, then click "Choose Home Page" (on the right side):

Layout Best For Modules Most K-12 teachers. Students land directly on your organized content. Pages Front Page If you want a custom welcome page with links, images, and instructions. Assignment List Shows upcoming assignments. Less common in K-12. Syllabus Shows the syllabus and upcoming calendar items. Course Activity Stream Shows recent activity. Can be overwhelming for younger students.

Recommended for K-12

Modules is the best default for most SCPS teachers. Students open the course and immediately see what they need to work on, organized in order. If you want a custom landing page with a welcome message and quick links, use Pages Front Page and link to your modules from there.

docs.title: Option A: Modules as your homepage
docs.text:
1. Go to Home → click "Choose Home Page".
  2. Select "Course Modules".
  3. Click "Set as Course Home Page".

Students will now land on your Modules page when they enter the course. Make sure your modules are organized and the current one is at the top.

docs.title: Option B: Custom front page
docs.text:
1. Go to Pages in the course navigation.
  2. Click "+ Page" to create a new page.
  3. Design your homepage using the Rich Content Editor:
    • Add a welcome message
    • Include a photo or banner
    • Add links to your current module, class expectations, or key resources
    • Embed a video introduction if you want
  4. Click "Save".
  5. On the saved page, click the three dots menu and select "Use as Front Page".
  6. Go to Home → "Choose Home Page" → select "Pages Front Page".

Keep it simple

For younger students (middle school), a clean page with 3–4 big, clear links works best. Don't overload the homepage with too much information — think of it as a table of contents, not an encyclopedia.

docs.title: Publishing your course
docs.text:
Your course is not visible to students until you publish it.

  1. Go to Home.
  2. Click the "Publish" button (or use the dropdown menu on the Home or Settings page).
  3. Students can now see and access the course.

Don't forget to publish

A common first-week issue: teachers set everything up but forget to publish the course. If students say they can't see your course, check this first.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Organizing content with modules — Build the structure behind your homepage
  • Creating an assignment — Add your first graded item

docs.title: Creating an Assignment
docs.text:
Assignments are the most common graded item in Canvas. This guide covers creating assignments with different submission types, due dates, and point values.

Time to complete: ~8 minutes

docs.title: Step 1: Start a new assignment
docs.text:
There are two ways to create an assignment:

From the Assignments pageFrom within a module
  1. Go to Assignments in the course navigation.
  2. Click "+ Assignment".
  1. Go to Modules, click the "+" on the module where you want the assignment.
  2. Select "Assignment" → "New Assignment".

docs.title: Step 2: Set up the basics
docs.text:
Field What to Enter Assignment Name Clear, descriptive name (e.g., Unit 3 Essay: Causes of the Revolution) Description Use the Rich Content Editor to write instructions, embed images/videos, and add links Points How many points the assignment is worth Assignment Group Which category it belongs to (e.g., Homework, Classwork, Assessments) Display Grade As Points, percentage, complete/incomplete, or letter grade

Write clear descriptions

Include everything a student needs: what to do, how to do it, what format to submit, and what success looks like. Students should be able to complete the assignment from the description alone without asking you a question.

docs.title: Step 3: Choose a submission type
docs.text: Submission Type What It Means Best For Online Students submit digitally through Canvas Most assignments On Paper No digital submission — you grade in person Handwritten work, physical projects No Submission No student submission expected Participation grades, in-class activities External Tool Students use a third-party tool MasteryConnect, Turnitin, etc.

docs.title: Online submission options
docs.text:
If you select Online, you'll see additional checkboxes:

  • Text Entry — Students type directly in Canvas
  • File Uploads — Students upload documents (Word, PDF, etc.)
  • Website URL — Students submit a link
  • Media Recordings — Students record audio or video
  • Student Annotation — Students annotate a document you provide

For Google-heavy classrooms

If students do their work in Google Docs, have them submit as a Website URL (paste the sharing link) or use the Google Assignments LTI if it's configured in your SCPS Canvas account.

docs.title: Step 4: Set due dates and availability
docs.text:
Field What It Controls Due Date When the assignment is due — submissions after this are marked late Available From When students can first see and access the assignment Until The last date students can submit (even late)

Due date vs. availability

The due date is the deadline. The Until date is when the submission window closes entirely. If you leave "Until" blank, students can submit late indefinitely (it will still be marked late).

docs.title: Differentiated due dates
docs.text:
Need different due dates for different students or sections?

  1. In the Assign section, click "+ Add".
  2. Search for a specific student, section, or group.
  3. Set unique due dates for each.

This is great for accommodations, makeups, or multi-section courses.

docs.title: Step 5: Save and publish
docs.text:
• Click "Save" to save as a draft (only you can see it).
  • Click "Save & Publish" to make it visible to students immediately.

You can also publish later from the Assignments page or Modules page.

docs.title: Assignment groups and weighting
docs.text: Assignments are organized into Assignment Groups (categories like Homework, Classwork, Tests). These groups can be weighted to calculate final grades.

docs.title: Setting up weighted grades
docs.text:
1. Go to Assignments.
  2. Click the three dots menu in the top right.
  3. Select "Assignment Groups Weight".
  4. Check "Weight final grade based on assignment groups".
  5. Enter the weight for each group (should total 100%).
Group Weight Classwork 30% Homework 20% Assessments 40% Participation 10%

Check your division's grading policy

SCPS may have specific grading weight requirements. Check with your building administration before setting up your own weighting scheme.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Building a quiz with New Quizzes — Create auto-graded assessments
  • Using rubrics — Attach a rubric for consistent grading
  • Using SpeedGrader — Grade submissions efficiently

docs.title: Managing the Gradebook
docs.text:
The Canvas Gradebook is where you view, manage, and organize all student grades. It calculates final grades automatically based on your assignment groups and weighting.

Time to complete: ~10 minutes

docs.title: Accessing the Gradebook
docs.text: Go to Grades in the course navigation. You'll see a spreadsheet-style view with students as rows and assignments as columns.

docs.title: Entering grades directly
docs.text:
You don't have to use SpeedGrader for everything. For simple grades:

  1. Click on any cell in the Gradebook.
  2. Type the score.
  3. Press Enter or Tab to save and move to the next student.

This is fast for entering participation points, in-class activity grades, or attendance-based scores.

docs.title: Column headers
docs.text:
Each column represents a graded item. Click the column header to see options:

  • Sort by score (high to low, low to high)
  • Message students who scored above/below a threshold, haven't submitted, etc.
  • Set default grade — assign the same score to all ungraded students
  • Post/hide grades — control when students see their scores
  • Open in SpeedGrader — jump to grading for that assignment

docs.title: Status indicators
docs.text: Indicator Meaning Orange dot Late submission Blue dot Resubmitted (student submitted again after grading) Dash (—) No submission Grayed out cell Assignment not assigned to this student Excused Student is excused from this assignment

docs.title: Marking an assignment as excused
docs.text:
1. Click the cell for the student/assignment.
  2. Type "EX" and press Enter.
  3. The assignment is marked as excused and won't count against the student's grade.

docs.title: Filter by section
docs.text: If you teach multiple sections in one course, use the section filter at the top of the Gradebook to view one section at a time.

docs.title: Filter by assignment group
docs.text: Click the "View" menu → "Arrange by" → select an assignment group to focus on just that category.

docs.title: Search for a student
docs.text: Use the search bar at the top to quickly find a specific student.

docs.title: Assignment groups and weighting
docs.text:
If you've set up assignment group weighting (see Creating an assignment), the Gradebook will show group totals and a final grade that reflects your weighting scheme.

The Total column on the far right shows each student's calculated course grade.

The Total column is powerful

This is the number that matters. Make sure your weighting is correct by checking a few students' grades manually. If the Total doesn't match what you expect, double-check your assignment group weights.

docs.title: Late policies
docs.text:
Canvas can automatically apply late penalties:

  1. Click the gear icon (⚙) in the Gradebook.
  2. Under "Late Policies":
    • Automatically apply grade for missing submissions: Set a default score (like 0%) for anything past due with no submission.
    • Automatically apply late submission deduction: Set a percentage to deduct per day late (e.g., 10% per day, up to a maximum deduction).

Check your school's late work policy

Before configuring automatic late penalties, make sure they align with your building or division's grading policy.

docs.title: Exporting grades
docs.text:
Need grades in a spreadsheet?

  1. Click "Export" in the Gradebook (top right).
  2. Select "Export Entire Gradebook".
  3. A CSV file downloads that you can open in Excel or Google Sheets.

This is useful for report cards, parent conferences, or data analysis.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Using SpeedGrader — Grade individual submissions
  • Creating an assignment — Add more graded items
  • Announcements and Inbox — Message students about their grades

docs.title: Creating a Graded Discussion
docs.text:
Graded discussions combine the engagement of a discussion board with accountability through a grade. They're excellent for assessing critical thinking, participation, and written communication.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Creating a graded discussion
docs.text:
1. Go to Discussions in the course navigation.
  2. Click "+ Discussion".
  3. Add a title and write your discussion prompt using the Rich Content Editor.
  4. Check the box "Graded" — this unlocks grading options.
  5. Set the points, assignment group, and due date.
  6. Click "Save & Publish" (or Save to keep as draft).

docs.title: Discussion settings to consider
docs.text:
Setting What It Does Recommendation Allow Threaded Replies Students can reply to each other's posts Turn on — creates real conversation Users Must Post Before Seeing Replies Students write their own response before seeing classmates' Great for original thinking — prevents copying Allow Liking Students can "like" posts Optional — adds engagement for older students Graded Adds the discussion to the gradebook Turn on for accountability Group Discussion Each group has their own discussion thread Use for small group activities

Require posting first

The "Must post before seeing replies" option is a game-changer. It ensures every student develops their own ideas before being influenced by classmates. Highly recommended for higher-order thinking prompts.

docs.title: Grading discussions
docs.text:
Graded discussions appear in SpeedGrader just like assignments:

  1. Open the discussion.
  2. Click "SpeedGrader" (or access it from the Gradebook).
  3. Read each student's posts and replies.
  4. Assign a score and leave feedback.

What counts as a 'submission'?

A student's discussion "submission" is their initial post. You'll see all their replies too, but the initial post triggers the submission in the gradebook.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Using rubrics — Attach a discussion rubric for consistent grading
  • Using SpeedGrader — Grade discussions efficiently

docs.title: Organizing Content with Modules
docs.text:
Modules are the backbone of your Canvas course. They let you organize content in a logical sequence — by unit, week, topic, or however you structure your instruction. Students work through modules in order, and you control what they see and when.

Time to complete: ~10 minutes

docs.title: What is a module?
docs.text:
A module is a container that holds a group of related items: pages, assignments, quizzes, discussions, files, and links. Think of it like a folder with a built-in sequence.

A typical module might look like:

Unit 3: The American Revolution

  • Overview and Learning Goals (Page)
  • Primary Source: Declaration of Independence (Link)
  • Reading Response (Assignment)
  • Unit 3 Quiz (Quiz)
  • Discussion: Was the Revolution inevitable? (Discussion)

docs.title: Creating a module
docs.text:
1. Go to Modules in the course navigation.
  2. Click "+ Module" in the top right.
  3. Give the module a name (e.g., Unit 3: The American Revolution or Week of Jan 13).
  4. Optionally set a lock date (students can't access until that date).
  5. Click "Add Module".

docs.title: Adding items to a module
docs.text:
1. Click the "+" button at the top of the module.

  2. Choose what to add:

    Item Type When to Use Assignment Select an existing assignment or create a new one Quiz Select an existing quiz File Upload or link to a document, PDF, or image Page A Canvas page with text, images, and embedded content Discussion A discussion board External URL A link to a website External Tool A third-party tool (like MasteryConnect, Diffit, etc.) Text Header A section divider within the module (great for organizing)

  3. Click "Add Item".

Use text headers

Text headers help break up large modules. For example, within a unit module, you might have headers for "Readings," "Activities," and "Assessment." This makes it easier for students to scan and find what they need.

docs.title: Reordering items and modules
docs.text:
• Drag and drop items within a module to reorder them.
  • Drag and drop entire modules to change the module order.
  • The current/active module should always be at the top (or clearly marked).

docs.title: Publishing modules and items
docs.text:
Modules and individual items have separate publish states:

  • A green checkmark means the item is published (visible to students)
  • A gray circle means it's unpublished (hidden from students)

To publish or unpublish:

  1. Click the publish icon (cloud/checkmark) next to the module name to publish the entire module and all its items.
  2. Click the publish icon next to an individual item to publish just that item.

Publish strategically

Only publish the current module and anything students need right now. Keep future modules unpublished. This prevents students from working ahead before you're ready, and keeps their view clean.

docs.title: Setting module requirements
docs.text:
You can require students to complete items in order before moving to the next:

  1. Click the three dots on the module and select "Edit".
  2. Under "Requirements", add completion criteria:
    • View the item — student must open it
    • Mark as done — student must click "Mark as Done"
    • Submit the assignment — student must submit work
    • Score at least X — student must reach a minimum score
  3. Optionally check "Students must complete requirements in order" for sequential progression.

Use requirements sparingly in K-12

Sequential requirements work well for self-paced content but can frustrate students if they get stuck on one item and can't access anything else. Consider using requirements for critical items only.

docs.title: Module best practices
docs.text:
Tips from your ITRT

  • Name modules clearly: Unit 3: The American Revolution is better than Module 3.
  • One module per unit or week: Don't create a module for every single day — it clutters the view.
  • Put the current module on top: Students should see what's relevant NOW first.
  • Include a "Start Here" module: At the top of your course, create a module with your syllabus, expectations, and a course orientation. It stays there all year.
  • Use Pages for content, not Files: A Canvas Page with embedded content looks better and is more accessible than asking students to download a file.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Creating an assignment — Build assignments to add to your modules
  • Building a quiz with New Quizzes — Add assessments to your modules
  • Setting up your course homepage — Point students to your modules

docs.title: Navigating the Canvas Dashboard
docs.text:
The Canvas dashboard is your starting point every time you log in. This guide walks you through the main areas so you can find what you need quickly.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Logging in
docs.text:
1. Go to your SCPS Canvas URL (bookmarked on your school devices).
  2. Sign in with your SCPS credentials (Google SSO or direct login, depending on your building's setup).

docs.title: The Dashboard
docs.text:
When you log in, the Dashboard shows your active courses as cards. Each card displays the course name, section, and a color you can customize.

What you can do from the Dashboard:

  • Click a course card to enter that course
  • View your To Do list on the right sidebar — upcoming items to grade
  • See recent activity across all courses
  • Use the Calendar (global navigation) to see all due dates across courses

Customize your cards

Click the three dots on any course card to nickname the course or change its color. Use colors that match your period schedule for quick visual identification.

docs.title: Global Navigation (left sidebar)
docs.text:
The left sidebar is always available no matter where you are in Canvas:

Icon What It Does Dashboard Returns you to your course card view Courses Lists all your current and past courses Calendar Shows all due dates, events, and assignments across courses Inbox Canvas messaging — send/receive messages to students, parents, and colleagues History Recently visited pages across all courses Commons Shared resource library — find and share course content

docs.title: Course Navigation (inside a course)
docs.text:
Once you click into a course, the left sidebar changes to show that course's navigation:

Menu Item What It's For Home The course landing page students see first Announcements Post class-wide messages Modules Your organized course content (the main student-facing view) Assignments All graded items — assignments, quizzes, discussions Grades The Gradebook People Your class roster Pages Content pages you've created Files Uploaded documents and media Discussions Discussion boards Quizzes Quiz management

Clean up your sidebar

Go to Settings → Navigation to hide menu items students don't need. A cleaner sidebar means less confusion. Most K-12 teachers keep Home, Modules, Assignments, Grades, and Announcements visible and hide the rest.

docs.title: Student View
docs.text:
Want to see what your course looks like from a student's perspective?

  1. Go to Settings (bottom of the course navigation).
  2. Click "Student View" on the right side.
  3. You'll see the course exactly as a student would.
  4. Click "Leave Student View" (top of the page) to return to your teacher view.

Test everything in Student View

Before launching a new assignment or module, always check Student View. It's the fastest way to catch broken links, hidden items, or confusing navigation.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Setting up your course homepage — Customize what students see first
  • Organizing content with modules — Build your course structure

docs.title: Building a Quiz with New Quizzes
docs.text:
New Quizzes is Canvas's modern quiz engine with more question types, item banks, and auto-grading features. This guide covers creating a quiz from scratch and from item banks.

Time to complete: ~10 minutes

docs.title: Creating a New Quiz
docs.text:
1. Go to Assignments → click "+ Assignment".
  2. Set the Submission Type to "External Tool".
  3. Select "New Quizzes" from the tool list.
  4. Set your points, due date, and assignment group.
  5. Click "Save" or "Save & Publish".
  6. You'll be taken into the New Quizzes editor to build your questions.

Alternatively, go to Quizzes → click "+ Quiz" → select "New Quizzes".

docs.title: Adding questions
docs.text: In the New Quizzes editor, click "+" to add a question. Available question types:

docs.title: Auto-graded types (most efficient)
docs.text: Type Description Multiple Choice One correct answer from a list Multiple Answer Select all that apply True/False Binary choice Matching Match items from two columns Ordering Arrange items in the correct sequence Fill in the Blank Students type a short answer (you define accepted answers) Hot Spot Students click on the correct area of an image Categorization Sort items into categories

docs.title: Manually graded types
docs.text:
Type Description Essay Open-ended written response File Upload Students submit a file as their answer Numeric Students enter a number (with tolerance range)

Mix your question types

A quiz with only multiple choice can be low-DOK. Mix in matching, ordering, and fill-in-the-blank to assess deeper understanding without needing manual grading.

docs.title: Using item banks
docs.text: Item banks are reusable collections of questions. You can pull random questions from a bank so each student gets a different version.

docs.title: Creating an item bank
docs.text:
1. In the New Quizzes editor, click "Item Banks" in the left sidebar.
  2. Click "+ Bank" and name it (e.g., Unit 3 - American Revolution).
  3. Add questions to the bank by clicking "+" inside the bank.

docs.title: Adding bank questions to a quiz
docs.text:
1. In your quiz, click "+" to add a question.
  2. Select "Item Bank" instead of creating a new question.
  3. Choose the bank and how many questions to pull.
  4. Canvas will randomly select questions from the bank for each student.

Why use item banks?

Item banks let you create a large pool of questions and randomly select a subset for each student. This reduces cheating and lets you reuse questions across multiple quizzes without rebuilding them.

docs.title: Quiz settings
docs.text:
Before publishing, configure your quiz settings:

Setting Recommendation Time Limit Set one for formal assessments; leave blank for practice quizzes Attempts 1 for summative; 2–3 for formative/practice Shuffle Questions Turn on to randomize question order per student Shuffle Answers Turn on to randomize answer choice order Show Results Choose when students see their results (after submission, after due date, or never) One Question at a Time Displays questions individually — good for preventing students from flipping ahead

docs.title: Accommodations
docs.text:
Need to give a student extra time or additional attempts?

  1. In the quiz settings, look for the "Manage Student Access" or "Moderate" option.
  2. Find the student's name.
  3. Add extra time (e.g., +15 minutes) or extra attempts.

This applies only to that student — the rest of the class keeps the default settings.

Set accommodations before students start

It's easier to set accommodations before the quiz opens. If a student has already started, you can still moderate, but it may require reopening their attempt.

docs.title: After the quiz
docs.text:
• Auto-graded questions will have scores available immediately.
  • Essay and file upload questions need to be scored manually in SpeedGrader.
  • View quiz statistics from the quiz page to see item analysis — which questions students missed most.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Creating a graded discussion — Another way to assess student thinking
  • Using SpeedGrader — Grade essay questions and provide feedback
  • Using rubrics — Attach rubrics to quiz essay questions

docs.title: Using Rubrics
docs.text:
Rubrics in Canvas provide clear grading criteria for students and make your grading faster and more consistent. You can attach rubrics to assignments, discussions, and quizzes.

Time to complete: ~8 minutes

docs.title: Creating a rubric
docs.text:
1. Open an assignment, graded discussion, or quiz.
  2. Click "+ Rubric" at the bottom of the page.

  3. Build your rubric:

    • Criteria (rows): What you're evaluating (e.g., "Thesis Statement," "Evidence," "Grammar")
    • Ratings (columns): Performance levels (e.g., "Excellent," "Proficient," "Developing," "Beginning")
    • Points: Assign point values to each rating level

  4. Click "Create Rubric" to save and attach it.

docs.title: Example rubric structure
docs.text: Criteria Excellent (4) Proficient (3) Developing (2) Beginning (1) Thesis Statement Clear, arguable thesis that addresses the prompt Thesis present and addresses the prompt Thesis is vague or partially addresses the prompt No clear thesis Use of Evidence 3+ relevant pieces of evidence with analysis 2 pieces of evidence with some analysis 1 piece of evidence, minimal analysis No evidence cited Organization Logical flow with clear transitions Generally organized with some transitions Some organization but hard to follow No clear organization

docs.title: Rubric options
docs.text:
When creating or editing a rubric, you'll see these options:

Option What It Does Use this rubric for assignment grading The rubric score becomes the assignment grade automatically Hide score total from students Students see individual criteria feedback but not the total — useful for feedback-only rubrics Free-form comments Lets you write custom comments for each criterion instead of selecting a pre-set rating

Use rubric for grading

Check "Use this rubric for assignment grading" to make grading a one-click process. Click the rating level for each criterion, and Canvas calculates the total automatically.

docs.title: Reusing rubrics
docs.text:
Once you create a rubric, you can reuse it across assignments:

  1. When adding a rubric to a new assignment, click "Find a Rubric".
  2. Search your course rubrics or browse by course.
  3. Select the rubric and attach it.

You can also modify a copy of an existing rubric without changing the original.

docs.title: Grading with rubrics in SpeedGrader
docs.text:
1. Open SpeedGrader for the assignment.
  2. The rubric appears on the right side.
  3. Click a rating level for each criterion.
  4. Add optional comments for each criterion.
  5. Click "Save" — the total score populates automatically.

This makes grading an essay or project dramatically faster than writing individual feedback from scratch every time.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Using SpeedGrader — Put your rubrics to work
  • Creating an assignment — Build assignments to attach rubrics to

docs.title: Using SpeedGrader
docs.text:
SpeedGrader is Canvas's built-in grading tool that lets you view student submissions, provide feedback, use rubrics, and assign scores — all in one streamlined interface.

Time to complete: ~8 minutes

docs.title: Accessing SpeedGrader
docs.text:
There are several ways to get to SpeedGrader:

  • From an assignment page: Click "SpeedGrader" in the right sidebar
  • From the Gradebook: Click on a cell, then click the SpeedGrader icon (or select "Open in SpeedGrader")
  • From Modules: Click the three dots on an assignment → "SpeedGrader"

docs.title: The SpeedGrader layout
docs.text:
SpeedGrader has three main areas:

Left side: Student list — navigate between students using the arrows or the dropdown

Center: The student's submission — you'll see their document, text entry, URL, or file

Right side: Grading tools — score field, rubric, and comments

docs.title: Assigning a score
docs.text:
1. Enter the score in the grade field (top right).
  2. Press Enter or Tab to save.
  3. Move to the next student with the arrow buttons.

docs.title: Using a rubric
docs.text:
If a rubric is attached:

  1. Click "View Rubric" on the right side.
  2. Click the appropriate rating level for each criterion.
  3. Add optional comments per criterion.
  4. Click "Save Rubric" — the total score auto-populates.

docs.title: Adding comments
docs.text:
1. Type feedback in the comments box on the right.
  2. You can also leave audio or video comments — click the media icon.
  3. Click "Submit" to save the comment.

Audio/video feedback

Recording a quick 30-second audio comment is often more meaningful than typed feedback and takes less time. Students appreciate hearing your voice, especially for complex assignments.

docs.title: Annotating submissions
docs.text:
For file uploads (Word docs, PDFs), you can annotate directly on the document:

  1. Use the annotation toolbar that appears above the submission.
  2. Tools include:
    • Highlight text
    • Strikethrough text
    • Add point comments (click anywhere to leave a note)
    • Free draw on the document
    • Add text boxes
  3. Annotations save automatically and are visible to the student.

docs.title: Navigating between students
docs.text:
• Use the left/right arrows at the top to move between students
  • Use the dropdown menu to jump to a specific student
  • Sort students by name, submission date, or grade using the settings icon
  • Filter to show only students who need grading

Filter for efficiency

Use the filter to show only ungraded submissions. This way you're not scrolling past students you've already graded.

docs.title: Posting grades
docs.text:
By default in many Canvas setups, grades may be hidden until you post them. This means students can't see their scores until you're ready.

  1. After grading, go to the Gradebook.
  2. Click the three dots on the assignment column.
  3. Select "Post Grades".
  4. Choose to post for everyone or only graded students.

Check your posting policy

Go to the Gradebook → assignment column → three dots → "Grade Posting Policy" to choose between Automatic (grades visible immediately) or Manual (you control when grades are visible).

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Managing the Gradebook — See all grades in one place
  • Using rubrics — Create rubrics to speed up grading
  • Creating an assignment — Build more assignments to grade

docs.title: Canvas Tips and Best Practices
docs.text:
A collection of power-user strategies, common mistakes to avoid, and SCPS-specific tips for getting the most out of Canvas.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Course design principles
docs.text:
Key principle

Design your Canvas course from the student's perspective. If a student opens your course and can't figure out what to do in 10 seconds, simplify.

docs.title: The 3-click rule
docs.text: Students should be able to find any current assignment within 3 clicks of opening your course. If they have to dig through menus and scroll through long lists, they'll disengage.

docs.title: Less is more
docs.text:
• Hide unused navigation items (Settings → Navigation)
  • Only publish the current module — keep future content hidden
  • Use consistent naming — Unit 3: Topic - Assignment Type is scannable
  • Avoid uploading files when a Page works better — Pages are mobile-friendly and accessible

docs.title: 1. Use modules as your single source of truth
docs.text: Put everything in modules in the order students should complete them. If an assignment lives in Modules, students always know where to look. Avoid scattering content across Assignments, Pages, and Files without a clear module structure.

docs.title: 2. Write self-contained assignment descriptions
docs.text: Every assignment description should answer: What do I do? How do I do it? When is it due? What does success look like? If a student has to ask you a question that's answered in the description, the description needs to be clearer.

docs.title: 3. Use "Message Students Who" religiously
docs.text: This Gradebook feature is the most underused tool in Canvas. Instead of guessing who's falling behind, let Canvas tell you. Message students who haven't submitted, scored below a threshold, or haven't been graded yet.

docs.title: 4. Copy courses between terms
docs.text:
Don't rebuild your course every semester.

  1. Go to Settings → Import Course Content.
  2. Select "Copy a Canvas Course".
  3. Choose the previous term's course.
  4. Select what to import (all content, specific modules, etc.).
  5. Update your due dates after import — this is the most common mistake.

docs.title: 5. Use Student View before publishing
docs.text: Always check Student View (Settings → Student View) before making content live. You'll catch broken links, unpublished items, and confusing navigation.

docs.title: Common mistakes to avoid
docs.text:
Things to watch out for

  • Not publishing the course — Students can't see anything until the course is published.
  • Publishing everything at once — Students get overwhelmed seeing 15 weeks of content. Publish one module at a time.
  • Forgetting to update due dates after course copy — Importing from last semester keeps the old dates. Update every one.
  • Not setting a grading policy — Check your assignment group weights before grades go home. One misconfigured weight can throw off every student's grade.
  • Using Files instead of Pages — Uploaded PDFs and docs are hard to read on Chromebooks and phones. Convert key content to Canvas Pages whenever possible.
  • Ignoring the To Do list — The To Do sidebar on your Dashboard shows ungraded submissions. Stay on top of it.

docs.title: SCPS-specific reminders
docs.text: Topic Details Course creation Courses are auto-created from your SIS roster. Don't create courses manually. MasteryConnect integration Your Canvas courses link to MasteryConnect trackers. See Linking MasteryConnect to Canvas. SIS grade sync Check with your admin about whether Canvas grades sync to your SIS (PowerSchool/other). This varies by building. Canvas Parent app Encourage families to download the Canvas Parent app for visibility into student grades and assignments. Canvas Student app Students can access Canvas on Chromebooks, phones, and tablets via the Canvas Student app. Test your assignments on mobile to ensure they work.

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text: I Want To... Do This Create an assignment Assignments → + Assignment Build a quiz Assignments → + Assignment → External Tool → New Quizzes Organize content Modules → + Module → add items Grade submissions Open assignment → SpeedGrader See all grades Grades (Gradebook) Message the class Announcements → + Announcement Message one student Inbox → Compose Message students who didn't submit Gradebook → column three dots → Message Students Who Check student view Settings → Student View Copy last semester's course Settings → Import Course Content Publish the course Home → Publish button

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
Explore the full set of Canvas guides:

  • Navigating the Canvas dashboard
  • Setting up your course homepage
  • Organizing content with modules
  • Creating an assignment
  • Building a quiz with New Quizzes
  • Creating a graded discussion
  • Using rubrics
  • Using SpeedGrader
  • Managing the Gradebook
  • Announcements and Inbox

docs.title: Diffit
docs.text: Diffit is an AI-powered tool that helps teachers create differentiated reading materials, comprehension questions, vocabulary lists, and activities from any text, topic, video, or website. It takes the heavy lifting out of differentiation so every student can access grade-level content at an appropriate reading level.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Guide Time Description Getting started with Diffit ~5 min Create your account and understand the interface

docs.title: Creating Resources
docs.text: Guide Time Description Generating differentiated reading passages ~8 min Create leveled texts from any topic, article, or video Questions, vocabulary, and activities ~8 min Auto-generated comprehension questions, vocab lists, and worksheets Exporting and sharing with students ~5 min Send to Google Docs, Slides, Forms, Classroom, PDF, and more

docs.title: Tips & Best Practices
docs.text:
Guide Time Description Diffit tips and best practices ~5 min Getting the most out of Diffit across subjects

SCPS-Specific Notes

  • Diffit offers a free tier with several resources per month. Check with your ITRT about whether SCPS has a premium license.
  • Diffit exports directly to Google Classroom, Google Docs, Google Slides, Google Forms, and PDF — all tools your students already use.
  • Always review AI-generated content for accuracy before sharing with students. Diffit is a time-saving tool, not a replacement for your expertise.
  • Diffit is especially valuable for ELL students and students with IEPs who need access to grade-level content at modified reading levels.

docs.title: Exporting and Sharing with Students
docs.text:
Once you've generated and customized your Diffit materials, you need to get them into students' hands. Diffit integrates with the tools your students already use.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Export options
docs.text:
Diffit supports direct export to:

Format Best For Google Docs Editable documents students can annotate or type responses into Google Slides Presentation-style delivery of content Google Forms Auto-graded quizzes from the comprehension questions Google Classroom Post directly as an assignment in your Google Classroom Microsoft 365 Word, PowerPoint, and other Microsoft formats PDF Printable worksheets for paper-based activities

docs.title: Exporting to Google Classroom
docs.text:
The fastest workflow for SCPS teachers:

  1. After generating your materials, click "Export" or "Send to".
  2. Select "Google Classroom".
  3. Choose the class and set it up as an assignment.
  4. The materials are automatically attached.
  5. Students see it in their Classwork page.

docs.title: Exporting to Google Forms (quizzes)
docs.text:
If you want the comprehension questions auto-graded:

  1. Select the questions you want to include.
  2. Export as "Google Forms".
  3. The form is created with questions and answer keys pre-populated.
  4. Assign through Google Classroom or share the form link.

docs.title: All exports stay editable
docs.text:
No matter which format you choose, the exported files remain fully editable. You can:

  • Add your own content alongside Diffit's generated material
  • Adjust formatting, add images, or rearrange sections
  • Mix Diffit materials from different passages into one document

Build a resource library

Save your exported Diffit materials in a Google Drive folder organized by unit. Over time, you'll build a library of differentiated resources you can reuse and share with colleagues.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Diffit tips and best practices — Get the most out of Diffit
  • Getting started with Diffit — Review the basics

docs.title: Getting Started with Diffit
docs.text:
Diffit turns any topic, article, or video into differentiated instructional materials in minutes. This guide walks you through setting up your account and understanding the basics.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Creating your account
docs.text:
1. Go to app.diffit.me.
  2. Click "Sign up" and create an account using your SCPS Google account (or email).
  3. Complete the brief setup — select your role (teacher), grade level, and subject area.

docs.title: The Diffit workflow
docs.text:
The basic flow is simple:

  1. Input — Give Diffit something to work with (a topic, article, video, or text)
  2. Configure — Choose the grade level, reading level, language, and standards
  3. Generate — Diffit creates differentiated materials
  4. Review & edit — Check the content and make adjustments
  5. Export — Send to Google Docs, Classroom, Forms, or download as PDF

docs.title: Ways to input content
docs.text: Input Method How It Works Topic Type any topic (e.g., "photosynthesis" or "causes of the Civil War") and Diffit generates reading material from scratch Article/URL Paste a web article URL and Diffit adapts it to your chosen reading level YouTube video Paste a YouTube link and Diffit creates a reading passage based on the video content Paste text Copy and paste any text you want adapted Upload a file Upload a document or PDF to be adapted

docs.title: Choosing your settings
docs.text:
Before generating, you'll configure:

Setting What to Choose Grade level 2nd grade through 12th grade and above Language English, Spanish, French, and more — great for ELL support Standards Optionally align to specific standards (Virginia SOLs, Common Core, etc.) Reading level Diffit adjusts text complexity to match your selection

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Generating differentiated reading passages — Create your first leveled text
  • Questions, vocabulary, and activities — See what Diffit auto-generates

docs.title: Questions, Vocabulary, and Activities
docs.text:
When Diffit generates a reading passage, it doesn't stop there. It automatically creates a full set of companion resources that are ready to use or customize.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: Auto-generated resources
docs.text: For every reading passage, Diffit creates:

docs.title: Comprehension questions
docs.text:
Type Description Multiple choice Standard recall and comprehension questions Short answer Students write brief responses Open-ended / essay Higher-order thinking prompts True/False Quick factual checks

You can adjust the DOK (Depth of Knowledge) level of questions to match your assessment goals.

docs.title: Vocabulary list
docs.text:
Diffit automatically pulls key vocabulary from the passage and provides:

  • Definitions appropriate to the reading level
  • Example sentences showing the word in context
  • The vocabulary list can be exported separately as a study tool

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: A concise overview of the passage's main ideas — useful as a reference sheet or study aid.

docs.title: Activities and worksheets
docs.text:
Diffit offers pre-built, adjustable activity templates:

Activity Description 3-2-1 Summary 3 things learned, 2 questions, 1 connection Graphic organizers Cause/effect, compare/contrast, main idea/details Vocabulary matching Drag-and-drop or match term to definition Discussion prompts Conversation starters based on the passage

Select the activities you want, and Diffit packages them with the reading.

docs.title: Customizing generated content
docs.text:
You're not locked into what Diffit creates. You can:

  • Edit any question — reword, adjust answer choices, or change difficulty
  • Remove questions that don't fit your goals
  • Add your own questions alongside the generated ones
  • Adjust vocabulary — add or remove words from the list
  • Change reading level and regenerate if the first version isn't quite right

Mix Diffit with your own materials

Diffit works best when you combine its generated content with your existing resources. Use Diffit for the differentiated passage and vocabulary, then add your own discussion questions or activity that ties to your specific lesson.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Exporting and sharing — Get everything to your students
  • Diffit tips and best practices — Subject-specific strategies

docs.title: Generating Differentiated Reading Passages
docs.text:
The core power of Diffit is creating reading passages at different levels from a single source. This guide covers how to create leveled texts for your classroom.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: Creating a passage from a topic
docs.text:
1. From the Diffit dashboard, choose "Topic" as your input.
  2. Enter the topic (e.g., "The water cycle").
  3. Select the grade level for the passage.
  4. Click "Generate".
  5. Diffit creates a reading passage at that level with key vocabulary highlighted.

docs.title: Creating multiple versions
docs.text:
To differentiate for your classroom, generate the same topic at multiple reading levels:

  • Create one version at your grade level (e.g., 7th grade)
  • Create another at a lower level for struggling readers or ELL students (e.g., 4th grade)
  • Create a higher version for advanced readers (e.g., 9th grade)

All three versions cover the same core content — students discuss the same topic but read at their level.

Same content, different levels

This is the magic of Diffit. A student reading at a 4th-grade level and a student reading at a 9th-grade level can participate in the same class discussion about the same topic. Neither student is excluded.

docs.title: Creating a passage from an article or video
From a web article
docs.text:
1. Select "Article" or paste a URL.
  2. Diffit reads the article and rewrites it at your chosen reading level.
  3. The core facts and ideas stay the same — only the language complexity changes.

docs.title: From a YouTube video
docs.text:
1. Select "YouTube video" and paste the video URL.
  2. Diffit transcribes and converts the video content into a reading passage.
  3. Choose the reading level and generate.

Turn videos into readings

This is incredibly useful. If you show a video in class but want students to have a text to reference later, Diffit can create one from the video in seconds.

docs.title: Refining your sources
docs.text:
Diffit lets you refine which sources inform the generated readings. You can:

  • Select or deselect sources that Diffit finds
  • Add your own context to guide the content
  • Adjust standards alignment to target specific learning objectives
  • Tune vocabulary to include or exclude specific terms

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Questions, vocabulary, and activities — See what comes with each passage
  • Exporting and sharing — Get the passages to your students

docs.title: Diffit Tips and Best Practices
docs.text:
Strategies for using Diffit effectively across subjects, plus common pitfalls to avoid.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: ELA
docs.text:
• Use Diffit to create leveled versions of the same text so all students can participate in the same discussion
  • Generate vocabulary lists from your current unit texts
  • Create comprehension questions at multiple DOK levels to differentiate assessment

docs.title: Science
docs.text:
• Paste a complex scientific article and have Diffit simplify it for your grade level
  • Use YouTube videos of experiments or phenomena as input — Diffit turns them into readable passages
  • Generate vocabulary lists for unit-specific scientific terminology

docs.title: Social Studies
docs.text:
• Input primary source documents and have Diffit create accessible versions while preserving key historical language
  • Create multiple reading levels of the same historical event for differentiated stations
  • Use the discussion prompts for Socratic seminar preparation

docs.title: Math
docs.text:
• Use Diffit for the word problem reading component — create leveled versions of word problems where the math stays the same but the reading complexity changes
  • Generate vocabulary lists for mathematical terms

docs.title: ELL / Multilingual Learners
docs.text:
• Generate passages in multiple languages — Diffit supports English, Spanish, French, and more
  • Create bilingual vocabulary lists with definitions in both languages
  • Use lower reading level passages to give ELL students access to grade-level content

docs.title: Common mistakes to avoid
docs.text:
Watch out for these

  • Not reviewing the content: Diffit uses AI, and AI makes mistakes. Always read through generated passages for factual accuracy.
  • Using only one reading level: The whole point is differentiation. Create at least 2–3 levels for each passage to serve your full range of learners.
  • Ignoring the vocabulary list: The auto-generated vocabulary is one of Diffit's most useful features. Use it for pre-teaching, word walls, or study guides.
  • Forgetting to align to standards: Diffit lets you select standards. Use this feature to ensure your materials are aligned to Virginia SOLs.
  • Replacing your own materials entirely: Diffit supplements your instruction — it doesn't replace your expertise, your texts, or your knowledge of your students.

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text: I Want To... Do This Create a leveled reading Choose topic or paste text → select grade level → Generate Make it easier for a struggling reader Regenerate at a lower grade level Get comprehension questions They're auto-generated with every passage Create a vocabulary study tool Export the vocabulary list to Google Docs or PDF Make an auto-graded quiz Export questions to Google Forms Assign to students Export to Google Classroom Create from a video Paste a YouTube URL as your input Support ELL students Generate in their home language or at a lower reading level

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
Explore the full set of Diffit guides:

  • Getting started with Diffit
  • Generating differentiated reading passages
  • Questions, vocabulary, and activities
  • Exporting and sharing with students

docs.title: Gemini for Workspace
docs.text: Gemini is Google's AI assistant built directly into the Google Workspace tools you already use — Docs, Gmail, Slides, Sheets, Forms, and Google Classroom. It can help you draft content, summarize documents, generate ideas, create images, and save time on repetitive tasks.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Guide Time Description What is Gemini and how to access it ~5 min Understanding Gemini's different forms and how to access them

docs.title: Gemini in Google Apps
docs.text: Guide Time Description Gemini in Docs ~8 min Draft, rewrite, and summarize content in Google Docs Gemini in Gmail ~5 min Summarize threads and draft replies faster Gemini in Slides ~5 min Generate images, create slides, and design presentations Gemini in Sheets and Forms ~5 min Create templates, organize data, and build assessments Gemini in Google Classroom ~8 min AI-powered lesson planning and resource creation

docs.title: Tips & Responsible Use
docs.text:
Guide Time Description Gemini tips and responsible use ~5 min Effective prompting, limitations, and ethical considerations

SCPS-Specific Notes

  • Gemini features are available to staff aged 18+ with SCPS Google Workspace accounts. Your admin controls which Gemini features are enabled.
  • Your data is protected: When using Gemini with your SCPS school account, your data is not used to train AI models and is covered under Google Workspace for Education terms.
  • Always review AI-generated content before sharing with students. Gemini is a drafting tool, not a finished-product generator.
  • Check with your ITRT about which Gemini features are currently enabled in your SCPS account — availability depends on your Workspace edition and admin settings.

docs.title: Gemini in Google Classroom
docs.text:
Gemini is now integrated directly into Google Classroom, providing AI-powered tools to help educators create resources, plan lessons, and differentiate content — all from one central location.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: Accessing Gemini in Classroom
docs.text:
1. Open Google Classroom.
  2. Look for the Gemini tab in the navigation bar.
  3. Click it to access AI-powered resource creation tools.

Availability

Gemini in Classroom is available to educators 18+ across all Google Workspace for Education editions. Your admin must have Gemini in Classroom enabled.

docs.title: What Gemini can do in Classroom
docs.text: Gemini in Classroom includes 30+ AI-powered tools for common educator tasks. Some highlights:

docs.title: Create first drafts of resources
docs.text:
• Lesson plans based on learning objectives
  • Vocabulary lists with definitions and example sentences
  • Reading passages adjusted to specific grade levels
  • Discussion prompts for class conversations
  • Rubrics for grading assignments
  • Study guides summarizing key concepts

docs.title: Differentiate content
docs.text:
• Take a complex text and adjust it to a different reading level
  • Create multiple versions of the same content for different learners
  • Generate materials that address common misconceptions

docs.title: Refine and iterate
docs.text: After Gemini generates content, you can ask it to refine the output in a chat-like experience: "Make it shorter," "Add more examples," "Adjust for ELL students."

docs.title: Assigning generated content
docs.text:
Once you've reviewed and edited Gemini's output:

  1. Click "Add to class".
  2. Select the class where you want to assign it.
  3. Classroom creates an assignment with the generated content attached as a Google Drive file.

Always review before assigning

Gemini creates drafts. Always read through the content, check for accuracy, adjust for your specific students, and make it your own before sending to students.

docs.title: Starter prompts
docs.text:
Gemini in Classroom provides pre-built prompt cards for common tasks like:

  • Gamifying an activity
  • Creating DOK (Depth of Knowledge) questions
  • Writing bell ringers or exit tickets
  • Generating differentiated reading materials

Click a card and Gemini loads a pre-set prompt that you can customize and iterate on.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Gemini tips and responsible use — Write better prompts and use AI responsibly
  • Gemini in Docs — Draft and refine content in Docs

docs.title: Gemini in Google Docs
docs.text:
Gemini in Docs acts as a writing partner — it can draft content from a prompt, rewrite existing text, summarize long documents, and help you get past a blank page.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: "Help me write" — drafting from scratch
docs.text:
1. Open a Google Doc.
  2. Click the Gemini sparkle icon in the toolbar (or look for "Help me write" in the left margin).
  3. Type a prompt describing what you want. Examples:
    • "Write a parent newsletter about our upcoming field trip to Luray Caverns"
    • "Draft a lesson plan for teaching the water cycle to 6th graders"
    • "Create a letter of recommendation template for a high school student"
  4. Click "Create".
  5. Gemini generates a draft. You can:
    • Insert it into your document
    • Regenerate for a different version
    • Refine by asking Gemini to make it shorter, more formal, more detailed, etc.

docs.title: Rewriting existing text
docs.text:
1. Select text in your document that you want to improve.
  2. Click the Gemini icon that appears.
  3. Choose an option:
    • Shorten — make it more concise
    • Elaborate — add more detail
    • Rephrase — reword for clarity
    • More formal / casual — adjust the tone
  4. Review the suggestion and accept or modify it.

docs.title: Summarizing documents
docs.text:
If you have a long document (meeting notes, report, article):

  1. Open the Gemini side panel (click the Gemini icon in the right sidebar).
  2. Type: "Summarize this document" or "What are the key points?"
  3. Gemini reads the document and provides a summary.

This is especially useful for quickly getting up to speed on long documents shared by your team or administration.

docs.title: Practical use cases for teachers
docs.text:
Task Prompt Example Parent communication "Write a brief email to parents about our class poetry reading on Friday" Lesson planning "Create a 5E lesson plan on photosynthesis for 9th grade biology" IEP meeting prep "Summarize this student progress document into key talking points" Rubric creation "Create a 4-point rubric for a persuasive essay" Sub plans "Draft substitute teacher plans for a day in my 7th grade math class covering fractions"

Always review and edit

Gemini drafts are starting points, not finished products. Always review for accuracy, tone, and alignment with your specific classroom context before sharing.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Gemini in Gmail — Summarize threads and draft replies
  • Gemini tips and responsible use — Write better prompts

docs.title: Gemini in Gmail
docs.text:
Gemini in Gmail helps you manage email faster by summarizing long threads and drafting replies.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Summarizing email threads
docs.text:
When you open a long email thread with many replies:

  1. Look for the "Summarize this email" button at the top of the thread (sparkle icon).
  2. Click it — Gemini reads all the messages and gives you a concise summary with key takeaways and any action items.

This is a lifesaver for threads with 10+ messages where you just need to know "what was decided?"

docs.title: Drafting replies with "Help me write"
docs.text:
1. Click "Reply" on an email.
  2. Click the Gemini sparkle icon in the compose toolbar (or look for "Help me write").
  3. Type a brief prompt: "Accept the meeting but ask to move it to Thursday" or "Politely decline and suggest next week."
  4. Gemini drafts a reply. Review, edit, and send.

You can also refine the draft: "Make it more formal" or "Shorten it."

docs.title: Smart reply suggestions
docs.text: Gemini enhances Gmail's smart reply suggestions with more contextual, relevant options based on the conversation. Look for suggested replies at the bottom of emails.

docs.title: Practical use cases
docs.text: Task Prompt Example Responding to parent emails "Write a professional reply acknowledging the concern and offering to schedule a conference" Replying to admin requests "Accept and confirm I'll have the data ready by Friday" Following up "Write a friendly follow-up asking if they had a chance to review my request"

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Gemini in Slides — Create presentation content
  • Gemini in Docs — Draft longer documents

docs.title: Gemini in Sheets and Forms
docs.text:
Gemini can help you create spreadsheet templates, organize data in Google Sheets, and build assessments in Google Forms.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Creating tables and templates
docs.text:
1. Open a Google Sheet.
  2. Click the Gemini icon or open the side panel.
  3. Describe what you need: "Create a grade tracker template with columns for student name, assignment 1 through 5, average, and letter grade."
  4. Gemini generates the structure. Insert it and customize.

docs.title: Organizing data
docs.text:
Gemini can help with data tasks:

  • "Create a formula to calculate the average of cells B2 through B30"
  • "Sort this data by the scores column from highest to lowest"
  • "Highlight cells in column C that are below 70"

Ask Gemini for formulas

If you're not a spreadsheet expert, Gemini can write formulas for you. Just describe what you want in plain English.

docs.title: Gemini in Google Forms
docs.text:
Gemini can help create assessments and surveys faster:

  1. Open Google Forms.
  2. Look for the Gemini feature (if available in your account).
  3. Describe what you need: "Create a 10-question quiz about the American Revolution for 8th graders with multiple choice and short answer questions."
  4. Gemini generates questions that you can review and edit.

Gemini can also generate a Form based on an existing Google Doc, Slide deck, or PDF from your Drive, and summarize form responses after students submit.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Gemini in Google Classroom — Use Gemini for lesson planning
  • Gemini tips and responsible use — Get better results from your prompts

docs.title: Gemini in Slides
docs.text:
Gemini in Google Slides can help you create slides, generate original images, and design presentations faster.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Creating slides with Gemini
docs.text:
1. Open a Google Slides presentation.
  2. Click the Gemini sparkle icon in the toolbar or open the side panel.
  3. Describe what you want: "Create a title slide for a presentation about the solar system" or "Add a slide summarizing the causes of World War I."
  4. Gemini generates slide content that you can insert and customize.

docs.title: Generating images
docs.text:
Gemini can create original images directly in your presentation:

  1. In the Gemini side panel, describe the image you want: "A colorful illustration of the water cycle for a science class."
  2. Gemini generates several image options.
  3. Click to insert your favorite into the slide.

Great for when you can't find the right clip art

Instead of searching for stock images, describe exactly what you need. The generated images are original and can be tailored to your lesson.

docs.title: Practical use cases
docs.text: Task What to Ask Gemini Building a presentation "Create an 8-slide presentation outline about the American Civil War for 8th graders" Adding visuals "Generate an image showing the three branches of government" Creating a summary slide "Create a summary slide with the 5 key takeaways from this presentation"

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Gemini in Sheets and Forms — Organize data and build assessments
  • Gemini in Docs — Draft supporting documents

docs.title: What Is Gemini and How to Access It
docs.text:
Gemini comes in several forms across Google's ecosystem. This guide clarifies what's what and how to access each one.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: The three faces of Gemini
docs.text: Form What It Is How to Access Gemini app A standalone AI chatbot (like ChatGPT) for brainstorming, research, and generating content Go to gemini.google.com or use the Gemini mobile app Gemini in Workspace AI features built into Docs, Gmail, Slides, Sheets, and Forms Look for "Help me write," "Help me create," or the Gemini sparkle icon inside each app Gemini in Classroom AI-powered lesson planning tools inside Google Classroom Open Google Classroom and look for the Gemini tab

docs.title: Accessing Gemini with your SCPS account
docs.text:
1. Make sure you're signed into your SCPS Google account (not a personal account).
  2. For the Gemini app: go to gemini.google.com.
  3. For Gemini in Workspace apps: open Docs, Gmail, Slides, etc. — look for the Gemini sparkle icon or "Help me write" prompts.

Feature availability

Not all Gemini features may be enabled for your SCPS account. If you don't see Gemini options in your apps, check with your ITRT — your admin may need to enable them.

docs.title: Data privacy
docs.text:
When using Gemini with your school account:

  • Your data is not used to train AI models
  • Your data is not reviewed by humans to improve Gemini
  • Gemini has the same enterprise-grade data protection as Gmail and Docs
  • It's covered under Google Workspace for Education terms, including FERPA and COPPA compliance

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Gemini in Docs — Start drafting and summarizing in Google Docs
  • Gemini in Google Classroom — Use AI for lesson planning

docs.title: Gemini Tips and Responsible Use
docs.text:
Getting the most out of Gemini requires good prompting skills and an understanding of its limitations. This guide covers both.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Writing effective prompts
docs.text: The quality of Gemini's output depends entirely on the quality of your prompt. Here's a framework:

docs.title: Be specific
docs.text: Vague Prompt Better Prompt "Write a lesson plan" "Write a 45-minute lesson plan on figurative language for 7th grade ELA, including a bell ringer, direct instruction, guided practice, and an exit ticket" "Make a quiz" "Create a 10-question multiple choice quiz on the water cycle for 5th graders, with DOK levels 1 and 2" "Help me write an email" "Write a professional email to parents informing them about the upcoming science fair, including the date (March 21), what students need to prepare, and a request for volunteers"

docs.title: Include context
docs.text:
Tell Gemini who the audience is, what grade level, what subject, and any constraints:

  • "For 9th grade biology students..."
  • "In language appropriate for ELL students at intermediate proficiency..."
  • "Following Virginia SOL standards for US History..."

docs.title: Iterate
docs.text:
Don't accept the first draft. Follow up with:

  • "Make it shorter"
  • "Add more examples"
  • "Adjust for a lower reading level"
  • "Make the tone more encouraging"
  • "Add a rubric"

docs.title: Limitations to understand
docs.text:
Gemini is not perfect

  • It can generate inaccurate information. Always fact-check, especially for historical dates, scientific details, and specific data.
  • It doesn't know your students. You know your classroom context, student needs, and school culture better than any AI.
  • It may not align perfectly with Virginia SOLs. Always verify standards alignment against the actual SOL framework.
  • It can sound generic. Add your personal voice and specific classroom references to make content feel authentic.

docs.title: For teachers
docs.text:
• Use Gemini as a starting point, not a finished product
  • Review everything before sharing with students, parents, or administration
  • Don't enter student PII (names, IDs, grades, IEP details) into Gemini prompts
  • Be transparent — if asked, be honest that you used AI to help draft content
  • Model good AI use for students — show them how you use it as a tool, not a replacement for thinking

docs.title: For student-facing content
docs.text:
• Always verify accuracy of any facts, dates, or data in generated content
  • Edit for your classroom context — add references to class discussions, local examples, and student interests
  • Check reading level — Gemini's estimate of reading level may not match your assessment

docs.title: Quick reference: Prompt templates
docs.text: Task Prompt Template Lesson plan "Create a [time] lesson plan on [topic] for [grade/subject], including [components]. Align to Virginia SOL [standard]." Parent email "Write a [tone] email to parents about [topic], including [key details]." Quiz "Create [#] [question types] questions on [topic] for [grade], at DOK levels [1/2/3]." Differentiated text "Rewrite this passage at a [grade] reading level, keeping the key concepts: [paste text]" Rubric "Create a [#]-point rubric for [assignment type] assessing [criteria]." Discussion prompt "Write [#] discussion questions about [topic] that encourage critical thinking for [grade]."

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
Explore the full set of Gemini guides:

  • What is Gemini and how to access it
  • Gemini in Docs
  • Gemini in Gmail
  • Gemini in Slides
  • Gemini in Sheets and Forms
  • Gemini in Google Classroom

docs.title: Google Classroom
docs.text: Google Classroom is the assignment and communication hub built into Google Workspace for Education. It integrates tightly with Google Docs, Sheets, Slides, Forms, and Drive, making it easy to create, distribute, collect, and grade student work — all within the Google ecosystem your students already know.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
New to Google Classroom or need a refresher?

Guide Time Description Navigating Google Classroom ~5 min Understand the Stream, Classwork, People, and Grades tabs Setting up your class ~8 min Configure class settings, grading system, and topics

docs.title: Creating & Managing Work
docs.text:
Assign and organize student work.

Guide Time Description Creating assignments ~8 min Build assignments with attachments, due dates, and points Creating quiz assignments with Google Forms ~8 min Auto-graded quizzes that sync to the Grades page Organizing classwork with topics ~5 min Group assignments by unit, week, or type Posting materials and questions ~5 min Share resources and quick-response questions

docs.title: Grading & Feedback
docs.text:
Grade student work and provide feedback.

Guide Time Description Grading and returning work ~8 min Grade from the student work page and the Grades tab Using the Grades page (Gradebook) ~5 min View and manage all grades in one place

docs.title: Communication
docs.text:
Stay connected with students and families.

Guide Time Description Stream, announcements, and comments ~5 min Post updates and manage class communication

docs.title: Tips & Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Guide Time Description Google Classroom tips and best practices ~5 min Power user strategies for K-12 teachers

SCPS-Specific Notes

  • Google Classroom classes may be auto-provisioned from your SIS or you may need to create them manually — check with your building ITRT.
  • Google Classroom rosters feed into Lightspeed Classroom for device monitoring. Make sure students have joined your Google Classroom before using Lightspeed.
  • SCPS uses Canvas as the primary LMS. Google Classroom is commonly used alongside Canvas for lightweight assignments, Google Docs workflows, and communication. Clarify with your building which platform is the primary gradebook of record.
  • Guardian summaries: Parents/guardians can receive email summaries of their student's work — encourage families to opt in.

docs.title: Creating Assignments
docs.text:
Assignments are the primary way students receive and submit work in Google Classroom. This guide covers creating assignments with attachments, due dates, and submission options.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: Creating a basic assignment
docs.text:
1. Go to the Classwork tab.
  2. Click "+ Create" → "Assignment".
  3. Fill in the details:
Field What to Enter Title Clear, descriptive name (e.g., Chapter 5 Reading Response) Instructions What students need to do — be specific Points Point value (or select "Ungraded") Due date When the assignment is due (date and optional time) Topic Which topic/category this belongs to (see Organizing with topics) Grade category If using weighted grading, select the category

docs.title: Attaching files and resources
docs.text:
Click "Add" to attach materials:

Attachment Type How It Works Google Drive Attach Docs, Sheets, Slides, or other Drive files Link Attach a URL to a website or resource File Upload a file from your computer YouTube Embed a YouTube video (if enabled by your admin)

docs.title: The three sharing options for Google Drive attachments
docs.text:
When you attach a Google Doc, Sheet, or Slide, you choose how students interact with it:

Option What Happens Best For Students can view file Read-only — students can see it but not edit Reference documents, readings, rubrics Students can edit file Everyone shares one copy and can edit it Collaborative documents, shared brainstorms Make a copy for each student Each student gets their own copy with their name on it Worksheets, templates, individual assignments

Make a copy for each student

This is the option you'll use most often. It creates a personal copy for every student, automatically named with their name. When they turn it in, you can grade each student's individual file.

docs.title: Creating files directly in the assignment
docs.text:
Click "Create" to make a new Google Doc, Sheet, Slide, Drawing, or Form right from the assignment. The new file is automatically attached.

This is great for creating a worksheet template on the fly — create it, and it's ready for students to use.

docs.title: Assigning to specific students
docs.text:
