By default, assignments go to all students. To assign to specific students:

  1. Under "For", click the dropdown showing "All students."
  2. Deselect "All students."
  3. Search for and select the specific students.

This is useful for differentiated assignments, makeups, or accommodations.

docs.title: Scheduling assignments
docs.text:
You don't have to post immediately:

  1. Instead of clicking "Assign", click the dropdown arrow next to it.
  2. Select "Schedule".
  3. Pick a date and time for the assignment to post automatically.

Plan your week in advance

Create all your assignments on Monday morning (or Sunday night) and schedule them to post throughout the week. Students get a steady drip of work without you having to be at your computer at specific times.

docs.title: Saving drafts
docs.text:
If you're not ready to post:

  1. Click the dropdown arrow next to "Assign."
  2. Select "Save draft".
  3. The assignment saves but is not visible to students.
  4. Return to it later from the Classwork tab to finish and post.

docs.title: Posting to multiple classes
docs.text:
1. Under "For", click the dropdown.
  2. Select additional classes.
  3. The same assignment posts to all selected classes.

Multiple classes share the same assignment

If you edit the assignment later, the change applies to all classes it was posted to. If you need different versions for different classes, create separate assignments.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Creating quiz assignments — Auto-graded quizzes with Google Forms
  • Organizing classwork with topics — Keep assignments organized
  • Grading and returning work — Grade what students turn in

docs.title: Using the Grades Page (Gradebook)
docs.text:
The Grades page gives you a spreadsheet view of all student grades across all assignments. You can enter grades, return work, and track missing assignments from here.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Accessing the Grades page
docs.text:
1. Open your class.
  2. Click the Grades tab at the top.

docs.title: Understanding the layout
docs.text:
• Rows = Students
  • Columns = Assignments (in the order they were created)
  • Overall grade = Shown on the far left (if you've set up a grading system)
  • Color coding: Red text = missing, Green text = turned in or draft grade

docs.title: Entering grades directly
docs.text:
1. Click any empty grade cell.
  2. Type the score.
  3. Press Enter — the grade saves as a draft.
  4. To return the grade to the student, click the three-dot menu on the cell → "Return".

docs.title: Sorting and filtering
docs.text:
• Click a column header to sort by that assignment's grades.
  • Use the grading period filter at the top if you've set up grading periods (quarters, semesters).
  • The overall grade column updates in real time as you enter grades.

docs.title: Exporting grades
docs.text:
1. On the Grades page, click the gear icon (Settings).
  2. Click "Copy all grades to Google Sheets" — this creates a Google Sheet with all current grades.
  3. You can also download grades from individual assignments.

Export at the end of each grading period

Create a Google Sheet snapshot of your grades before the end of each quarter. This gives you a backup and a record you can share with your administration if needed.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Grading and returning work — Grade individual submissions
  • Setting up your class — Configure grading categories and weights

docs.title: Grading and Returning Work
docs.text:
When students turn in work, it shows up for you to grade. Grades are saved as drafts until you return the assignment, at which point students can see their score.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: Viewing student submissions
docs.text:
1. Go to the Classwork tab.
  2. Click on the assignment.
  3. Click the number above "Turned in" to open the Student Work page.

You'll see all students organized by status:

Status Meaning Color Turned in Student submitted their work — Assigned Student hasn't submitted yet — Graded You've entered a grade — Missing Past due with no submission Red

docs.title: Grading from the Student Work page
docs.text:
1. Click on a student's name to open their submission.
  2. Their attached file opens in the right panel — you can review it directly.
  3. Enter a grade in the grade field on the right.
  4. Optionally add a private comment for the student.
  5. The grade saves as a draft until you return it.

docs.title: Adding comments and feedback
docs.text:
• Private comments: Only the student sees these. Use for individual feedback.
  • Annotation (on Google Docs): Open the student's Google Doc and add comments or suggestions directly on the document. Students see your comments when the assignment is returned.

Comment directly on the Doc

For Google Docs submissions, open the doc and use the commenting and suggesting tools built into Google Docs. This is more specific and actionable than a general comment in the grade field.

docs.title: Returning work
docs.text: Grades are invisible to students until you return the assignment.

docs.title: Return to one student
docs.text:
1. On the Student Work page, check the box next to the student's name.
  2. Click "Return".
  3. Confirm.

docs.title: Return to multiple students
docs.text:
1. Check the boxes next to multiple students (or select all).
  2. Click "Return".
  3. All selected students receive their grade and any feedback.

Students are notified

When you return work, students receive a notification (if they have notifications enabled). They can see their grade and your comments.

docs.title: Bulk grading with "Grade All"
docs.text:
For assignments graded on completion (exit tickets, classwork):

  1. Go to the Grades tab.
  2. Click the three-dot menu on the assignment column.
  3. Click "Grade all".
  4. Enter the grade to apply to all students (e.g., 100).
  5. Optionally check "Override existing grades" and "Automatically return after grading".
  6. Click "Apply".

Huge time saver

For completion-based assignments, "Grade All" lets you give every student the same score in one click. Combined with "Automatically return," the entire grading and returning process takes 5 seconds.

docs.title: Marking work as missing, excused, or complete
docs.text:
• Missing: Automatically applied when work is past due with no submission. You can also manually mark it.
  • Excused: Removes the assignment from the student's grade calculation. Use for absent students or accommodations.
  • Complete: Marks the assignment as done without a numeric grade.

To apply these: click the grade cell on the Grades page → use the dropdown options.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Using the Grades page — Manage all grades in one view
  • Creating assignments — Create more work to grade

docs.title: Posting Materials and Questions
docs.text:
Not everything in Classroom needs to be a graded assignment. Materials let you share resources, and Questions let you gather quick student responses.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Posting materials
docs.text:
Materials are ungraded resources — readings, reference documents, videos, links.

  1. Go to Classwork → "+ Create" → "Material".
  2. Add a title and optional description.
  3. Attach files, links, Drive documents, or YouTube videos.
  4. Select a topic to keep things organized.
  5. Click "Post".

Materials don't appear in the Grades page and don't have due dates. They're purely reference content.

docs.title: When to use materials vs. assignments
docs.text: Use a Material Use an Assignment Sharing a study guide Completing a worksheet Posting a video to watch Responding to a video with questions Sharing a link to a resource Submitting work based on the resource

docs.title: Posting questions
docs.text:
Questions are lightweight, graded or ungraded prompts that collect a quick student response.

  1. Go to Classwork → "+ Create" → "Question".
  2. Choose the question type:
    • Short answer — Students type a response
    • Multiple choice — Students select from options you provide
  3. Add the question text.
  4. Set points (or ungraded), due date, and topic.
  5. Options:
    • Students can reply to each other — turns it into a mini-discussion
    • Students can edit their answer — allows revision
  6. Click "Ask".

docs.title: When to use questions
docs.text:
Scenario Question Type Exit ticket Short answer: "What's one thing you learned today?" Quick poll Multiple choice: "Which topic should we review tomorrow?" Bell ringer Short answer: "Define [vocabulary term] in your own words." Check for understanding Multiple choice with a content question

Questions are great for daily engagement

A quick question at the start or end of class takes 2 minutes to create and gives you immediate insight into student thinking — without the overhead of a full assignment.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Creating assignments — For work that needs full submissions
  • Organizing classwork with topics — Keep all content organized

docs.title: Navigating Google Classroom
docs.text:
Google Classroom is organized into four main tabs per class. Understanding each one will help you work efficiently.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Accessing Google Classroom
docs.text:
1. Go to classroom.google.com or click the Classroom icon in your Google Apps waffle menu.
  2. Sign in with your SCPS Google account.
  3. You'll see your class cards on the homepage.

docs.title: The four tabs
docs.text: When you open a class, you'll see four tabs across the top:

docs.title: Stream
docs.text: The Stream is the activity feed — like a social media timeline for your class. Announcements appear here, and students see notifications when new assignments are posted. You can post quick updates, but the Stream is not the best place to organize classwork.

docs.title: Classwork
docs.text: The Classwork tab is where you create and organize all assignments, quizzes, questions, and materials. This is the tab students should use to find their work. Content is organized by topics (your categories).

docs.title: People
docs.text:
The People tab shows your class roster — students and any co-teachers. From here you can:

  • Invite or remove students
  • Email students or guardians
  • Mute individual students from posting on the Stream

docs.title: Grades
docs.text: The Grades tab is your gradebook. It shows all graded assignments as columns and students as rows. You can enter, edit, and return grades directly from this view.

docs.title: Class card customization
docs.text:
On the homepage, each class appears as a card. You can:

  • Change the header image — click the class card, then click "Customize" at the bottom right of the banner
  • Reorder classes — drag and drop cards to arrange them by period

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Setting up your class — Configure grading and class settings
  • Creating assignments — Start posting work

docs.title: Organizing Classwork with Topics
docs.text:
Topics are folders that group your assignments, quizzes, and materials on the Classwork page. Without topics, everything piles up in one long list.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Creating a topic
docs.text:
1. Go to the Classwork tab.
  2. Click "+ Create" → "Topic".
  3. Name the topic (e.g., Unit 3: The American Revolution or Week 5).

docs.title: Choosing a topic structure
docs.text:
Approach Example Topics Best For By unit Unit 1: Foundations, Unit 2: Growth, Unit 3: Conflict Content-driven courses with clear units By week Week 1, Week 2, Week 3 Courses with consistent weekly cadence By type Classwork, Homework, Assessments, Resources When you want work grouped by category Hybrid Unit 3 - Classwork, Unit 3 - Assessments Best of both worlds

Most K-12 teachers prefer by unit or by week

Students find it easiest when the Classwork page mirrors how class time is structured. If you teach in units, use unit topics. If you follow a weekly rhythm, use week topics.

docs.title: Assigning a topic to classwork
docs.text:
When creating any assignment, quiz, or material:

  1. In the creation form, find the "Topic" dropdown.
  2. Select an existing topic or create a new one right there.

You can also drag and drop existing assignments between topics on the Classwork page.

docs.title: Reordering topics and items
docs.text:
• Drag topics up or down to reorder them. Put the current topic at the top.
  • Drag items within or between topics to reorganize.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Creating assignments — Create work to organize into topics
  • Posting materials and questions — Add non-graded content

docs.title: Creating Quiz Assignments with Google Forms
docs.text:
Google Forms integrates directly with Classroom to create auto-graded quizzes. Scores sync automatically to your Grades page.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: Creating a quiz assignment
docs.text:
1. Go to Classwork → "+ Create" → "Quiz assignment".
  2. A Google Form is automatically created and attached.
  3. Fill in the assignment title, instructions, points, due date, and topic.
  4. Click the Google Form attachment (called "Blank Quiz") to open the Forms editor.

docs.title: Building your quiz in Google Forms
Adding questions
docs.text:
1. Click "+" to add a new question.
  2. Choose a question type:
Type Auto-Graded? Best For Multiple choice Yes Standard quiz questions Checkbox Yes "Select all that apply" Short answer Yes (with answer key) Fill-in-the-blank, single-word answers Paragraph No — needs manual grading Open-ended responses Dropdown Yes Select from a list

docs.title: Setting up the answer key
docs.text:
1. At the bottom of each question, click "Answer key".
  2. Select the correct answer(s).
  3. Set the point value for the question.
  4. Optionally add answer feedback — different messages for correct and incorrect responses.

Answer feedback is powerful

Write a brief explanation for incorrect answers: "Not quite — remember that the Constitution was ratified in 1788, not 1776." Students see this immediately after submitting, turning every wrong answer into a learning moment.

docs.title: Quiz settings in Google Forms
docs.text:
Click the gear icon (Settings) in Google Forms:

Setting Recommendation Release grade "Immediately after each submission" for formative; "Later, after manual review" for summative Respondent can see Choose what students see after submitting: missed questions, correct answers, point values Limit to 1 response Turn on for formal assessments Shuffle question order Turn on to reduce copying

docs.title: How grades sync to Classroom
docs.text:
When students complete the quiz:

  1. Auto-graded questions are scored immediately.
  2. Grades appear on the Grades page in Classroom.
  3. You can also import grades from Forms to Classroom: open the assignment → click "Import Grades".

For paragraph/open-ended questions, you'll need to grade those manually in Google Forms and then import the updated grades.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Grading and returning work — Review and return quiz results
  • Creating assignments — Create non-quiz assignments

docs.title: Setting Up Your Class
docs.text:
Before posting assignments, take a few minutes to configure your class settings. This ensures grades calculate correctly and students have a smooth experience.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: Accessing class settings
docs.text:
1. Open your class.
  2. Click the gear icon (Settings) in the top right corner.

docs.title: Configuring your grading system
docs.text:
Under "Grading" in Settings, choose how overall grades are calculated:

Option How It Works Best For Total Points Total points earned ÷ total points possible = overall grade Simple grading — all assignments weighted equally by their point value Weighted by Category You assign percentage weights to grade categories Weighting categories differently (e.g., Tests worth more than Homework) No Overall Grade No grade is calculated for students When you don't want students seeing an overall percentage

docs.title: Setting up weighted categories
docs.text:
If you choose Weighted by Category:

  1. Click "Add Grade Category".
  2. Name the category (e.g., Classwork, Assessments, Homework).
  3. Assign a percentage weight.
  4. Repeat for all categories. Weights must total 100%.
  5. Click "Save".

Match your school's grading policy

Make sure your categories and weights match what your building requires. Check with your administration before setting up your own system.

docs.title: Other important settings
docs.text: Setting What to Do Class code Students use this to join your class. You can reset or disable it after everyone has joined. Stream Choose who can post and comment on the Stream. "Only teacher can post or comment" keeps it clean. Classwork on Stream Choose whether classwork posts appear on the Stream. Turning this on keeps students informed; turning it off keeps the Stream clean. Show deleted items Leave this off unless you need to recover something. Guardian summaries Enable guardian email summaries so parents receive updates about their student's work.

docs.title: Inviting students
docs.text:
There are two ways to add students:

Class codeEmail invite
  1. Share the class code (found in Settings or on the Stream banner).
  2. Students go to classroom.google.com → click "+" → "Join class" → enter the code.
  1. Go to the People tab.
  2. Click the invite icon next to "Students."
  3. Enter student email addresses.

docs.title: Inviting guardians
docs.text:
Guardians receive email summaries of their student's upcoming and missing work.

  1. Go to the People tab.
  2. Next to a student's name, click the three dots → "Invite guardian".
  3. Enter the guardian's email address.

Encourage guardian opt-in

Send a note home at the start of the year asking families for their email addresses for Google Classroom guardian summaries. This dramatically reduces "I didn't know about the assignment" conversations.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Creating assignments — Start posting work for students
  • Organizing classwork with topics — Set up your content structure

docs.title: Stream, Announcements, and Comments
docs.text:
Google Classroom offers several ways to communicate with students. The Stream is your class activity feed, and you can control how students interact with it.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: The Stream
docs.text: The Stream is the first tab students see when they open your class. It shows recent activity: new assignments, announcements, and comments.

docs.title: Posting an announcement
docs.text:
1. At the top of the Stream, click "Announce something to your class" (or "+" on mobile).
  2. Type your message.
  3. Optionally attach files, links, Drive documents, or YouTube videos.
  4. Click "Post" to send immediately, or click the dropdown to schedule it for later.

Announcements are great for reminders, updates, and sharing resources that don't need a grade.

docs.title: Controlling Stream permissions
docs.text:
In Class Settings (gear icon), you can control who can post:

Setting What It Means Students can post and comment Full open discussion — can get noisy Students can only comment Students can reply to your posts but can't create their own Only teachers can post or comment Clean, teacher-controlled feed

Recommended for most K-12 classes

"Students can only comment" is usually the sweet spot. You control the conversation topics, but students can still ask questions and engage with your posts.

docs.title: Private comments vs. Stream comments
docs.text:
Type Who Sees It Where to Use It Stream comments Everyone in the class General questions, class discussions Private comments Only you and the student Individual feedback, grade questions, personal matters

Students can leave private comments on assignments. You can reply privately from the Student Work page.

docs.title: Emailing students and guardians
docs.text:
From the People tab:

  1. Check the box next to a student's name (or select multiple).
  2. Click "Actions" → "Email".
  3. This opens your Gmail with the selected recipients pre-filled.

For guardians, you can invite them to receive guardian email summaries — automatic weekly or daily digests of their student's upcoming and missing work.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Setting up your class — Configure Stream permissions and guardian summaries
  • Google Classroom tips and best practices — Communication strategies

docs.title: Google Classroom Tips and Best Practices
docs.text:
A collection of power-user strategies, common mistakes to avoid, and SCPS-specific tips.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: 1. Use topics religiously
docs.text: A Classwork page without topics is a disaster for students. Create topics before you start posting, and assign every item to a topic. Keep the current topic at the top by reordering.

docs.title: 2. "Make a copy for each student" is your best friend
docs.text: For worksheets, templates, and graphic organizers, always select "Make a copy for each student" when attaching a Google Doc. Each student gets their own copy, it's automatically named with their name, and when they turn it in, you can grade it individually.

docs.title: 3. Schedule assignments in advance
docs.text: Build your week's assignments in one sitting and schedule them to post on the right days. This saves time and ensures students get a consistent flow of work.

docs.title: 4. Use "Grade All" for completion-based work
docs.text: Exit tickets, bell ringers, classwork checks — use the "Grade All" feature in the Grades tab to give everyone the same score at once. Paired with "Automatically return," it takes seconds.

docs.title: 5. Check "Turned in" counts before class ends
docs.text: Before students leave, glance at the Classwork page to see how many have turned in the assignment. Call out the count: "I see 18 out of 24 turned in — if you haven't submitted, do it now." This catches stragglers in real time.

docs.title: 6. Copy your class for new semesters
docs.text:
Don't rebuild from scratch every term:

  1. On the Google Classroom homepage, click "+" → "Create class".
  2. Or go to an existing class → gear icon → find the option to copy the class.
  3. All assignments, materials, and topics carry over.
  4. Update due dates — this is the one thing that doesn't transfer cleanly.

docs.title: Common mistakes to avoid
docs.text:
Watch out for these

  • Not using topics: Students see one long, unsorted list. They'll miss things.
  • Choosing "Students can edit file" when you meant "Make a copy": One student edits the doc and everyone's copy changes. Always double-check the sharing option.
  • Forgetting to return work: Grades are invisible until you return them. Students think they haven't been graded.
  • Posting to the Stream instead of Classwork: The Stream is a feed — things scroll away. Assignments and materials belong on the Classwork page.
  • Not setting up grading categories before posting assignments: If you add categories later, you'll need to go back and assign categories to every existing assignment.
  • Leaving the class code open all year: After all students have joined, disable or reset the class code to prevent unauthorized joins.

docs.title: Google Classroom vs. Canvas at SCPS
docs.text:
Since SCPS uses both platforms, here's when to use which:

Use Google Classroom For Use Canvas For Quick assignments using Google Docs/Sheets/Slides Formal course structure with modules Daily classwork and bell ringers Summative assessments and New Quizzes Simple grading with "Grade All" Weighted gradebook with detailed reporting Classes that live entirely in the Google ecosystem MasteryConnect integration and standards-based tracking Lightweight communication via Stream SpeedGrader annotation and detailed rubric feedback

Know your primary gradebook

Check with your building administration about which platform is the gradebook of record — the one where official grades live. Don't split grades between two systems without a clear plan.

docs.title: Quick reference
docs.text: I Want To... Do This Create an assignment Classwork → + Create → Assignment Create a quiz Classwork → + Create → Quiz assignment Share a resource Classwork → + Create → Material Ask a quick question Classwork → + Create → Question Make an announcement Stream → "Announce something" Grade work Click assignment → student work page → enter grade Grade all at once Grades tab → three-dot menu → "Grade all" Return work Student work page → check students → Return Export grades Grades → Settings → Copy all grades to Google Sheets Invite guardians People → three dots on student → Invite guardian

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
Explore the full set of Google Classroom guides:

  • Navigating Google Classroom
  • Setting up your class
  • Creating assignments
  • Creating quiz assignments
  • Organizing classwork with topics
  • Posting materials and questions
  • Grading and returning work
  • Using the Grades page
  • Stream, announcements, and comments

docs.title: Lightspeed Classroom
docs.text: Lightspeed Classroom Management gives you real-time visibility into what students are doing on their devices during class. You can monitor screens, control web access, send messages, push links, and keep students focused — all from your own device.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
New to Lightspeed Classroom? Start here.

Guide Time Description Logging in and navigating the dashboard ~5 min Access Lightspeed and understand the main interface Starting and managing a class session ~5 min Use custom and ad hoc schedules to activate monitoring

docs.title: Monitoring & Engagement
docs.text:
Keep students on task and engaged.

Guide Time Description Viewing student screens ~5 min Monitor individual screens or the whole class at once Sending messages to students ~5 min Send nudges, check-ins, and announcements to devices Pushing links to students ~3 min Share URLs directly to student browsers Sharing your screen ~3 min Broadcast your screen to the class

docs.title: Web Rules & Access Control
docs.text:
Control what students can access during class.

Guide Time Description Setting up allow and block lists ~8 min Restrict or open web access for your class Creating student groups with custom rules ~8 min Differentiate web access by group Locking screens and pausing internet ~3 min Temporarily freeze devices for transitions or direct instruction

docs.title: Tips & Troubleshooting
docs.text:
Guide Time Description Using the AccessScan extension ~5 min Check if websites are blocked for students before planning lessons Lightspeed tips and best practices ~5 min Power user strategies from your ITRT

SCPS-Specific Notes

  • Lightspeed Classroom syncs with your Google Classroom rosters. Make sure your Google Classrooms are set up and students have joined before using Lightspeed.
  • Student devices are only monitored during active class sessions on campus. Teachers cannot monitor students outside of school hours or off campus.
  • If you find a site that needs to be allowed or blocked at the district filter level (not just your class), submit a ticket through your building's IT process.

docs.title: Using the AccessScan Chrome Extension
docs.text:
The Lightspeed AccessScan extension lets you check whether a website or specific page is accessible to your students before you plan a lesson around it. It scans the page and shows you exactly which elements are allowed and which are blocked by the district content filter.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: What is AccessScan?
docs.text:
AccessScan is a Chrome extension pushed out to all SCPS teacher accounts. It lets you browse to any website, run a scan, and see — from a student's perspective — what's allowed and what's blocked.

This is different from your classroom Allow/Block lists in Lightspeed Classroom. AccessScan checks the district-level content filter, which applies to all students regardless of your class web rules.

Why this matters

Just because YOU can see a website doesn't mean your students can. Teacher and student filter settings are different. A site that loads fine on your account might be completely blocked for students. Always check with AccessScan before planning a lesson around a new website.

docs.title: Step 1: Navigate to the website
docs.text: Open Chrome (signed into your SCPS Google account) and go to the website you want to check. For example: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/American_Revolution

docs.title: Step 2: Open the AccessScan extension
docs.text:
1. Look for the Lightspeed AccessScan icon in your Chrome extension bar (top right of the browser — it looks like a light blue link/chain icon).
  2. If you don't see it, click the puzzle piece icon to show all extensions and find AccessScan.
  3. Click the AccessScan icon.

docs.title: Step 3: Enter a student's email
docs.text:
1. In the AccessScan popup, type a student's email address (e.g., jsmith@shenandoah.k12.va.us).
  2. Click "Scan Page".

docs.title: Step 4: Review the results
docs.text:
AccessScan will show you a list of every resource on that page and whether it's:

Status What It Means Allowed Students can access this resource Blocked This resource is blocked by the district filter

If any critical elements are blocked (the page itself, embedded videos, login redirects), your students won't have a good experience — even if the main URL appears accessible.

docs.title: What to do if a site is blocked
docs.text:
If AccessScan shows that a site you need is blocked:

  1. Click "Email results" in the AccessScan window (if available).
  2. AccessScan generates a summary of what's blocked.
  3. Click "Copy to Clipboard".
  4. Open your email and paste the results into a new message.
  5. Send the request to your building's IT support (follow your school's ticket process for filter requests).
  6. Include the URL, your reason for needing access, and the date you need it by.

Plan ahead

Submit unblock requests at least a few days before you plan to use the site. Filter changes may need admin approval and won't happen instantly.

docs.title: Common scenarios
docs.text: Scenario What to Do Planning a lesson with a new website Scan it first. If it's blocked, submit a request before building the lesson around it. YouTube video is blocked Check the specific video URL. Individual videos may be blocked even if YouTube itself is allowed. Consider creating a YouTube playlist and sharing it with IT for bulk unblocking. Embedded content doesn't load for students The main site may be allowed but embedded resources (fonts, scripts, CDNs) might be blocked. Scan the page to see all resources. Site works on your teacher device but not student devices Your teacher filter settings are more permissive. AccessScan shows you the student experience.

docs.title: Important notes
docs.text:
AccessScan vs. your Lightspeed Classroom web rules

  • AccessScan checks the district-level content filter — the baseline filter that applies to all students everywhere.
  • Lightspeed Classroom Allow/Block lists are your class-level rules that layer on top of the district filter.
  • Even if you allow a site in your Lightspeed Classroom Allow List, it will still be blocked if the district filter blocks it. AccessScan helps you identify these conflicts.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Setting up allow and block lists — Your class-level web rules
  • Tips and best practices — More Lightspeed strategies

docs.title: Setting Up Allow and Block Lists
docs.text:
Web Rules are one of Lightspeed Classroom's most useful features. They let you control exactly which websites students can access during your class. There are two modes: Allow (whitelist) and Block (blacklist).

Time to complete: ~8 minutes

docs.title: Understanding the two modes
docs.text:
Mode What It Does Best For Allow Mode Students can ONLY visit sites on your list — everything else is blocked Testing, focused assignments, research with specific sources Block Mode Students can visit any district-approved site EXCEPT the ones on your list General class time when you just want to block known distractors

Which should I use?

Allow Mode is more restrictive and works best when students should only be on one or two specific sites. Block Mode is more permissive and works well for general class time. When in doubt, start with Block Mode and switch to Allow Mode for focused activities.

docs.title: Setting up an Allow List
docs.text:
1. From your class view, click "Web Rules" at the top of the page.
  2. Select the "Allow Mode" tab.
  3. Click "New Allow List" (or select an existing one).
  4. Give the list a name (e.g., Research Project Sites or Math Practice Only).
  5. Add website URLs one per line. Only include the domain — for example:
    • google.com (not https://www.google.com)
    • docs.google.com
    • khanacademy.org
  6. Press Enter after each URL to add it as a separate entry.
  7. Click "Select List" to activate it.
  8. Click "Apply Lists".

Don't forget SSO

If your school uses Google Single Sign-On, check the "Allow Single Sign On (SSO)" checkbox in the advanced options. Without this, students may not be able to log into Google-based tools even if you've allowed them, because SSO routes through hidden URLs that might not be on your list.

docs.title: Setting up a Block List
docs.text:
1. From your class view, click "Web Rules".
  2. Select the "Block Mode" tab.
  3. Click "New Block List" (or select an existing one).
  4. Name the list (e.g., Common Distractors).
  5. Add URLs of sites you want to block:
    • youtube.com
    • tiktok.com
    • coolmathgames.com
    • discord.com
  6. Click "Select List" to activate it.
  7. Click "Apply Lists".

docs.title: Managing your lists
docs.text:
• You can create up to 20 Allow Lists and 20 Block Lists
  • Each list can contain up to 100 URLs
  • Toggle lists on and off without deleting them — great for switching between activities
  • Lists are per class — you'll need to set up lists separately for each class (or reuse lists you've already created)

docs.title: Checking web rules status at a glance
docs.text:
From your class view, the Web Rules link at the top shows the current status:

Display Meaning Web Rules Off No allow or block lists are active Allow Mode An allow list is currently active Block Mode A block list is currently active

docs.title: Common Allow List setups
docs.text: Here are some ready-to-use list ideas:

docs.title: Testing mode
docs.text:
docs.google.com
forms.google.com

docs.title: Research project
docs.text:
docs.google.com
scholar.google.com
britannica.com
worldbookonline.com

docs.title: Math practice
docs.text:
khanacademy.org
deltamath.com
desmos.com
docs.google.com

Build a library of lists

Create lists for your common activities and just toggle them on/off as needed. You don't have to rebuild them every time.

docs.title: Turning off web rules
docs.text:
1. Click "Web Rules".
  2. Select "Off" at the top of the panel.
  3. Students return to normal district-filtered browsing.

docs.title: The "Done" status and web rules
docs.text:
Lightspeed has a feature where students can mark themselves as "Done" with an assignment. When a student's status is set to Done:

  • Web Rules are automatically released for that student
  • They can browse freely (within district filter limits) while waiting for others to finish

This is configurable — ask your ITRT if this feature is enabled for your building.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Creating student groups with custom rules — Give different groups different access
  • Locking screens and pausing internet — For when you need full attention
  • Pushing links to students — Pair with Allow Mode for focused activities

docs.title: Starting and Managing a Class Session
docs.text:
Lightspeed Classroom only monitors students during an active class session. This guide covers how to start, manage, and end sessions using the different schedule types.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Understanding schedule types
docs.text:
Lightspeed offers three ways to run a class session:

Schedule Type What It Does Best For Bell Schedule Automatically starts/stops based on your school's bell times Set by your district — no action needed from you Custom Schedule You set specific start and end times Regular class periods you want to run the same time each day Ad Hoc You choose a duration and start it immediately Study halls, flex periods, one-off sessions

At SCPS

Check with your ITRT about whether bell schedules have been configured for your building. If not, you'll use custom or ad hoc schedules.

docs.title: Option A: Using a custom schedule
docs.text:
Custom schedules let you set a recurring time for your class session.

  1. Click on a class from your My Classes homepage.
  2. Click Settings (gear icon) at the bottom left.
  3. Select "Custom" schedule.
  4. Set the start time and end time for your class.
  5. Click "Save Settings".

The session will automatically start and stop at those times each school day.

One custom schedule per class per day

You can only set one custom schedule per class. If you teach the same group at two different times, use the custom schedule for one and an ad hoc session for the other.

docs.title: Option B: Using an ad hoc session
docs.text:
Ad hoc sessions are perfect for flexible schedules or one-time monitoring needs.

  1. Click on a class from your My Classes homepage.
  2. Click the "Start Class" button (or look for the ad hoc schedule option).
  3. Select a duration (e.g., 30 minutes, 45 minutes, 1 hour).
  4. Click "Start" — monitoring begins immediately.

The session will automatically end when the timer runs out, or you can stop it manually.

docs.title: Ending a session early
docs.text:
If class ends early or you need to stop monitoring:

  1. Open the class you want to stop.
  2. Click "End Class" or stop the ad hoc timer.
  3. Student devices are released from monitoring.

Don't forget to end your session

If your session is still running when the next teacher tries to monitor the same students, those students will show up as "Available to Add" in the other teacher's class. End your session promptly to avoid this.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
My custom schedule isn't starting automatically
  • Go to Settings → Custom and verify the times are correct
  • Make sure you clicked "Save Settings"
  • Try refreshing the page
  • If the issue persists, contact your ITRT — there are occasional known issues with custom schedules
Students aren't showing up in my class
  • Make sure the students have joined your Google Classroom (Lightspeed syncs from Google Classroom rosters)
  • Verify the student's device is online and on campus
  • Check if the student is in "Available to Add" — another teacher's session may still be active

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Viewing student screens — Now that your session is running, learn to monitor
  • Setting up allow and block lists — Control web access during your session

docs.title: Locking Screens and Pausing Internet
docs.text:
Sometimes you need every eye off a screen and on you. Lightspeed lets you lock student screens or disable internet access with one click.

Time to complete: ~3 minutes

docs.title: Locking all screens
docs.text:
1. From your class view, click the "Lock Screens" button in the top toolbar.
  2. Every student's device displays a lock screen message — they cannot interact with their device.
  3. Click "Unlock Screens" when you're ready for them to resume.

docs.title: When to lock screens
docs.text: Scenario Why It Works Giving instructions No distractions — every student sees the lock screen Transitioning between activities Students close laptops mentally when screens are locked Class discussion Removes the temptation to multitask Taking attendance or making announcements Quick way to get attention

docs.title: Locking one student's screen
docs.text:
1. Click on the student's thumbnail.
  2. Click the "Lock" button in their individual view.
  3. Only that student's device is locked — the rest of the class continues working.
  4. Click "Unlock" when you're ready.

The individual lock

This is a great alternative to taking a device away. If a student is persistently off-task, lock their screen for a few minutes while you have a quiet conversation with them. It's less disruptive than collecting the Chromebook.

docs.title: Closing off-task tabs
docs.text:
Instead of locking the whole screen, you can surgically close specific tabs:

  1. Click on a student's thumbnail to see their open tabs.
  2. Find the off-task tab.
  3. Click the "X" or "Close" button next to that tab.
  4. The tab closes on the student's device.

The student keeps working on their other tabs — you just removed the distraction.

docs.title: Disabling internet access
docs.text:
Lightspeed can also fully disable internet access for a student or the whole class:

  1. Look for the "Disable Internet" or "Pause Internet" option.
  2. Toggle it on for the class or individual students.
  3. Students' devices remain on but cannot access any websites.
  4. Toggle it off to restore access.

Lock screens vs. disable internet

  • Lock screens: The student sees a lock message and can't do anything on the device.
  • Disable internet: The student can still use offline apps and documents but can't browse the web.

Choose based on whether you want them completely stopped (lock) or just offline (disable internet).

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Tips from your ITRT

  • Give a verbal heads-up before locking screens: "Screens are going to lock in 10 seconds — save your work." This prevents lost work and frustrated students.
  • Use lock screens briefly — 2–3 minutes for instructions, then unlock. Extended locking can feel punitive.
  • Close tabs before escalating to a lock. Give the student a chance to self-correct.
  • Pair with screen sharing. Lock screens → share your screen → demonstrate → unlock screens → students work.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Sharing your screen — Broadcast your screen while devices are locked
  • Sending messages to students — Try a message before a lock
  • Viewing student screens — Monitor after unlocking

docs.title: Logging In and Navigating the Dashboard
docs.text:
The Lightspeed Classroom dashboard is your command center for monitoring and managing student devices. This guide walks you through accessing it and understanding the layout.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Step 1: Log in to Lightspeed Classroom
docs.text:
1. Open your browser and go to classroom.relay.school (or use the Lightspeed link in your bookmarks bar).
  2. Click "Sign in with Google" and use your SCPS Google account.
  3. You'll land on the My Classes homepage.

Bookmark it

Add classroom.relay.school to your bookmarks bar so it's always one click away.

docs.title: Step 2: Understand the My Classes homepage
docs.text:
The My Classes page shows all of your classes as tiles. Each tile represents a class synced from Google Classroom.

What you can do from here:

  • Click a class tile to open it and start monitoring
  • Drag and drop tiles to reorder them in whatever way makes sense for your schedule
  • See student status at a glance — each tile shows how many students are online

docs.title: Class tile status indicators
docs.text: Indicator Meaning Students shown with screen thumbnails Class session is active, students are being monitored Grayed out tile No active session for this class Number badge Count of students currently online in that class

docs.title: Step 3: Know the key areas of a class view
docs.text: Once you click into a class, here's what you'll see:

docs.title: Top toolbar
docs.text:
The toolbar across the top gives you quick access to the main actions:

  • Web Rules — Set allow/block lists for the class
  • Share Screen — Broadcast your screen to students
  • Lock Screens — Freeze all student devices
  • Push Link — Send a URL to student browsers
  • Message — Send a message to student devices

docs.title: Student tile area
docs.text:
The main area shows a thumbnail of each student's screen in real time. You can:

  • Zoom in/out using the slider to see more or fewer screens at once
  • Click on a student to zoom into their individual screen
  • Right-click a student (or click the three dots) for individual actions

docs.title: Student status sections
docs.text:
Students are organized into sections:

Section Meaning My Class Students currently checked into your class and being monitored Available to Add Students who should be in your class but are checked into another teacher's session Offline Students whose devices are not currently online

Available to Add

If a student shows up in "Available to Add," it usually means another teacher's session is still running. You can drag and drop them into your class to take over monitoring.

docs.title: Step 4: Add a custom class (optional)
docs.text:
If you need to monitor students who aren't in one of your Google Classrooms (e.g., a study hall, advisory, or after-school group):

  1. Hover over the space under My Classes on the homepage.
  2. Click "Add Class".
  3. Name the class.
  4. Add students by email address, or share a join code/link with them.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Starting and managing a class session — Activate monitoring for your class
  • Viewing student screens — Learn to monitor effectively
  • Setting up allow and block lists — Control web access

docs.title: Sending Messages to Students
docs.text:
Lightspeed lets you send instant messages directly to student devices — a quiet, non-disruptive way to redirect behavior, check in, or make announcements.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Sending a message to the whole class
docs.text:
1. From your class view, click the "Message" button in the top toolbar.
  2. Make sure "Entire Class" is selected.
  3. Type your message or choose from a predefined message (e.g., "Are you done?" or "Need help?").
  4. Click "Send".

The message appears as a notification on every student's screen. Students can respond with "Yes" or "No".

docs.title: Sending a message to one student
docs.text:
1. Click on the student's thumbnail to open their individual view.
  2. Click the "Message" icon.
  3. Type a custom message or select a predefined one.
  4. Click "Send".

The quiet redirect

This is one of the most powerful features in Lightspeed. Instead of calling out a student in front of the class, send a quiet message: "Hey — I see you're not on the assignment. Need help?" It preserves the student's dignity and keeps class moving.

docs.title: Sending a message to a group
docs.text:
If you've created student groups (see Creating student groups):

  1. Select the group from the left sidebar.
  2. Click "Message".
  3. Your message will go to only the students in that group.

docs.title: Predefined messages
docs.text:
Lightspeed includes predefined messages you can send with one click. Common options include:

  • "Are you done?"
  • "Need help?"
  • "Please stay on task"
  • "Eyes up here"

These are great for quick check-ins without needing to type.

Student responses

When a student receives a message, they see it as a browser notification and can tap "Yes" or "No". Their response shows up on your dashboard next to their name.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Tips from your ITRT

  • Use messages before locking screens or closing tabs. Give the student a chance to self-correct first.
  • Keep messages short and kind. "Need help getting started?" works better than "Get on task."
  • Use predefined messages for speed. During a busy class, one-tap messages save time.
  • Check for responses. If a student says "Yes" to "Need help?", follow up in person.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Pushing links to students — Share URLs directly to their browsers
  • Viewing student screens — Monitor who needs a message
  • Creating student groups — Target messages to specific groups

docs.title: Pushing Links to Students
docs.text:
Instead of writing a URL on the board or telling students to "go to this website," Lightspeed lets you push a link directly to student browsers. One click from you, and every student is on the same page — literally.

Time to complete: ~3 minutes

docs.title: Push a link to the whole class
docs.text:
1. From your class view, click the "Push Link" button in the top toolbar.
  2. Paste or type the URL you want students to visit.
  3. Select "Entire Class" (or choose specific students/groups).
  4. Click "Send".

The link opens automatically in a new tab on every student's device.

docs.title: Push a link to one student or a group
docs.text:
1. For one student: Click on their thumbnail, then click the Push Link icon.
  2. For a group: Select the group from the left sidebar, then click Push Link.
  3. Enter the URL and send.

docs.title: When to use Push Link
docs.text:
Scenario Why Push Link Helps Starting a new activity Gets everyone to the same page instantly — no time wasted typing URLs Sharing a Google Doc or Form No need to post a link in Google Classroom first Redirecting a student Push the correct resource to a student who's on the wrong site Differentiated resources Push different links to different groups

Pair with Allow Mode

Push the link first, then turn on Allow Mode so students can only access that site (and any others you've allowed). This keeps them focused on exactly what you want.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Sharing your screen — Show students what's on your screen
  • Setting up allow and block lists — Lock down access after pushing links

docs.title: Sharing Your Screen
docs.text:
Screen sharing in Lightspeed broadcasts what's on your screen to all student devices — useful for demonstrations, walkthroughs, or getting everyone's attention.

Time to complete: ~3 minutes

docs.title: How to share your screen
docs.text:
1. From your class view, click the "Share Screen" button in the top toolbar.
  2. Choose what to share (your entire screen or a specific browser tab, depending on your setup).
  3. Click "Start Sharing".
  4. Students will see your screen displayed on their devices.
  5. Click "Stop Sharing" when you're done.

docs.title: When to use screen sharing
docs.text:
Scenario How It Helps Walking through a website or tool Students follow along on their own screens Modeling an assignment Show exactly what you expect Reviewing as a class Display a document or presentation

Combine with Lock Screens

For maximum attention, lock student screens first (see Locking screens), then share your screen. This prevents students from clicking away while you're presenting.

docs.title: Student-to-class screen sharing
docs.text:
Lightspeed also allows you to broadcast a student's screen to the rest of the class — great for student presentations or sharing exemplary work.

  1. Click on the student's thumbnail.
  2. Select "Share to Class" (or similar option).
  3. The student's screen is now visible to everyone.
  4. Stop sharing when the student is finished.

Get permission first

Always ask the student before sharing their screen with the class. You don't want to accidentally broadcast something personal or embarrassing.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Locking screens and pausing internet — Pair with screen sharing for focused instruction
  • Pushing links to students — Send resources after your demonstration

docs.title: Creating Student Groups with Custom Rules
docs.text:
Student groups in Lightspeed let you apply different web rules, push different links, and send messages to subsets of your class. This is perfect for differentiated instruction, group projects, or tiered interventions.

Time to complete: ~8 minutes

docs.title: Why use groups?
docs.text:
Without groups, every web rule you set applies to the entire class. With groups, you can:

  • Give your research group access to the internet while your testing group is locked to one site
  • Push different links to different groups working on different activities
  • Send targeted messages to just the students who need a redirect

docs.title: Creating a group
docs.text:
1. Open your class in Lightspeed.
  2. Hover over the space under "My Class" on the left sidebar.
  3. Click "Create Group".
  4. Name the group (e.g., Table 1, Research Group, Intervention, Testing).
  5. Click "Save".

Your new group appears in the left sidebar below "My Class."

docs.title: Adding students to a group
docs.text:
1. From the main class view, find the student you want to add.
  2. Drag and drop the student's tile from "My Class" into the group on the left sidebar.
  3. Repeat for all students in that group.

You can also drag students between groups or back to My Class to reorganize.

Quick setup

Set up your groups at the beginning of the period before students start working. It takes 30 seconds to drag a few students into groups, and it saves you time switching rules later.

docs.title: Setting group-specific web rules
docs.text:
Each group has its own Group Web Rules that work independently from the class rules.

  1. Click on the group name in the left sidebar to select it.
  2. Click "Group Web Rules" (or look for the web rules option within the group).
  3. Set up Allow or Block lists just for that group (see Allow and block lists for details).
  4. Apply the list.

docs.title: Example: Two groups, two sets of rules
docs.text:
Group Activity Web Rule Research Group Working on a research paper Allow Mode: docs.google.com, scholar.google.com, britannica.com Testing Group Taking a quiz Allow Mode: forms.google.com only

Students in each group only see the sites allowed for their group.

docs.title: Group-specific actions
docs.text:
With a group selected, you can also:

  • Push a link to just that group
  • Send a message to just that group
  • Lock screens for just that group
  • Share your screen to just that group

This makes it easy to run multiple activities simultaneously.

docs.title: Removing groups
docs.text:
When you no longer need a group:

  1. Drag all students back to "My Class".
  2. The empty group can be deleted or left for reuse next time.

Groups reset between sessions

Groups you create are tied to the class, but you may need to re-add students each session depending on your setup. Some teachers create their standard groups at the start of each week.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Tips from your ITRT

  • Name groups clearly: Table 1 or Red Group is fine for quick grouping. Intervention - Standard 3.2 is better for targeted instruction.
  • Use groups for MTSS: If you're running tiered interventions, groups let you give Tier 2 students access to specific practice sites while Tier 1 students work independently.
  • Combine with Push Link: Push the assignment link to each group separately so each group lands exactly where they need to be.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Setting up allow and block lists — Build the lists you'll apply to groups
  • Pushing links to students — Send different resources to different groups
  • Sending messages to students — Target messages by group

docs.title: Lightspeed Tips and Best Practices
docs.text:
A collection of power-user strategies, common mistakes to avoid, and SCPS-specific tips for getting the most out of Lightspeed Classroom.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: The Lightspeed mindset
docs.text:
Key principle

Lightspeed is a teaching tool, not a surveillance tool. The goal is to support learning, not to catch students doing something wrong. Use it to redirect, differentiate, and engage — not to police.

docs.title: 1. Tell students you use it
docs.text: The single most effective strategy is transparency. On the first day, show students what you see. Demonstrate the screen monitoring, the message feature, and the tab history. When students know the tool exists, off-task behavior drops dramatically — and you barely have to use it.

docs.title: 2. Message before you lock
docs.text:
When you see a student off-task, try this escalation:

  1. Send a message — "I see you're not on the assignment. Need help?"
  2. Close the tab — remove the distraction
  3. Lock their screen — only if the behavior continues
  4. Talk to the student in person — private conversation

Most students self-correct at step 1.

docs.title: 3. Build your Allow Lists in advance
docs.text: Don't wait until class starts to set up your web rules. Before the week begins, build out your Allow Lists for each major activity. Then it's just a toggle during class.

docs.title: 4. Use groups for stations or rotations
docs.text: If you run station-based learning, create a group for each station. Push the correct link and set the correct web rules for each group. Students rotate through stations and their access changes with them.

docs.title: 5. Check history, not just live screens
docs.text: A quick scan of browsing history at the end of class tells you more than watching screens all period. You can see patterns of off-task behavior over time and address them in a targeted conversation.

docs.title: Common mistakes to avoid
docs.text:
Things to watch out for

  • Leaving a session running after class ends. This blocks the next teacher from monitoring those students. Always end your session.
  • Using Allow Mode without enabling SSO. Students won't be able to log into Google tools, and you'll get a flood of "it's not working" complaints.
  • Setting web rules for the whole class when only one student needs them. Use individual actions or groups instead.
  • Over-locking screens. Students lose engagement and momentum when locked too long or too often. Use it sparingly.
  • Forgetting to include docs.google.com in Allow Lists. Almost every assignment involves Google Docs — if it's not on the list, students can't work.

docs.title: SCPS-specific reminders
docs.text: Topic Details Roster sync Lightspeed syncs from Google Classroom. If a student isn't showing up, check their Google Classroom enrollment first. Off-campus monitoring Teachers cannot monitor students outside of school hours or off campus. The district filter still works everywhere, but Lightspeed Classroom is school-only. District filter vs. your rules Your Allow/Block lists work on top of the district filter. You can restrict access further than the district filter, but you can't unblock something the district has blocked. Requesting district-level changes If a site needs to be allowed or blocked at the district level, submit a ticket through your building's IT process. Lightspeed AccessScan This Chrome extension (added to teacher accounts) lets you check if a specific URL is allowed or blocked by the district filter before class. Use it to avoid surprises. See the full AccessScan guide.

docs.title: Quick reference: Feature cheat sheet
docs.text: I Want To... Do This See all student screens Open your class — they're right there Zoom in on one student Click their thumbnail Send a message Top toolbar → Message Push a link Top toolbar → Push Link Share my screen Top toolbar → Share Screen Lock all screens Top toolbar → Lock Screens Block a website Web Rules → Block Mode → add URL Allow only specific sites Web Rules → Allow Mode → add URLs Create a student group Hover under My Class → Create Group Check browsing history Click student → History Close an off-task tab Click student → find tab → Close

docs.text:
