docs.title: Viewing Student Screens
docs.text:
Real-time screen monitoring is the core feature of Lightspeed Classroom. This guide covers how to view the whole class, zoom into individual students, and check browsing history.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Viewing the whole class
docs.text: When you open an active class, you'll see a grid of student screen thumbnails — small live previews of what every student is currently seeing on their device.

docs.title: Adjust the view
docs.text:
Use the zoom slider to adjust how many screens you see at once:

  • Zoomed out: See all students at a glance — great for a quick scan of the room
  • Zoomed in: See fewer students but with larger, more readable thumbnails

The quick scan

At the start of class, zoom out to see everyone. Quickly scan for any screens that look obviously off-task (games, YouTube, social media). Then zoom in on those specific students.

docs.title: Viewing an individual student
docs.text:
Click on any student's thumbnail to zoom into their screen. From the individual student view, you can:

  • See their full screen in real time (on Windows/Mac, this shows the entire desktop, not just the browser)
  • View their open tabs listed below the screen
  • Check their browsing history for the class period
  • Send them a message directly
  • Close specific tabs that are off-task
  • Lock their screen individually

docs.title: Checking browsing history
docs.text:
1. Click on a student's name or thumbnail.
  2. Select "History" to see every site they've visited during the current class session.
  3. This is useful for verifying whether a student was on task even when you weren't watching.

docs.title: Understanding screen thumbnail indicators
docs.text:
Not every student's screen will display a clear thumbnail. Here's what you might see:

What You See What It Means Clear screen thumbnail Student is online and being monitored normally "Thumbnail unavailable" Student has a new blank tab open or is on a page that can't be captured Grayed out / offline icon Student's device is not connected Screen recording icon You are recording this student's screen

Chromebook vs. Windows/Mac

On Chromebooks, Lightspeed can only see the browser window. On Windows and Mac devices, Lightspeed can see the full desktop — including other applications. This means Chromebook students could potentially use Android apps without being seen in Lightspeed.

docs.title: Recording a student's screen
docs.text:
If a student is persistently off-task and you need documentation:

  1. Click on the student's thumbnail to open their individual view.
  2. Click the "Record" button.
  3. The screen will be recorded for the duration of the class session (or until you stop it).
  4. The recording is saved and can be shared with parents or administration.

Privacy note

Screen recording is limited to the active class session only. Recordings are meant for documenting off-task behavior for parent/admin conversations. Use this feature thoughtfully and professionally.

docs.title: Best practices for screen monitoring
docs.text:
Tips from your ITRT

  • Don't sit at your desk and stare at Lightspeed all period. Use it as a tool, not a replacement for walking the room. A quick glance every few minutes is enough.
  • Let students know you can see their screens. The awareness alone reduces off-task behavior dramatically.
  • Use the zoom slider strategically. Start zoomed out for a scan, then zoom in on students who need attention.
  • Check history, not just live screens. A student might close a tab before you see it live, but the history doesn't lie.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Sending messages to students — Redirect students without disrupting the class
  • Locking screens and pausing internet — Freeze devices for transitions
  • Setting up allow and block lists — Proactively control access

docs.title: MasteryConnect
docs.text: MasteryConnect is the standards-based assessment and data platform used across SCPS to track student mastery of Virginia SOL standards. It integrates with Canvas to provide a seamless assessment experience.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
New to MasteryConnect? Start here.

Guide Time Description Setting up your Mastery Tracker ~10 min Create your first tracker and add standards Linking MasteryConnect to Canvas ~5 min Connect your tracker to your Canvas course

docs.title: Assessments
docs.text:
Everything you need to create, administer, and score assessments.

Guide Time Description Creating an assessment ~10 min Build an assessment from scratch or the item bank Using the item bank ~8 min Find and use pre-built, standards-aligned questions Administering assessments to students ~5 min Generate testing codes and manage student access Scoring with bubble sheets ~5 min Print, scan, and score paper-based assessments

docs.title: Data & Reports
docs.text:
Turn assessment data into instructional decisions.

Guide Time Description Reading the Mastery Tracker ~8 min Understand the color-coded tracker display Running reports ~8 min Generate item analysis, student, and standards reports Using data to group students ~10 min Create intervention and enrichment groups from data

SCPS-Specific Notes

  • Trackers must be linked through Canvas for student access and reporting consistency.
  • MVPA (Mastery View Predictive Assessments) are pushed out from the district — you do not need to create these yourself.
  • Questions you author go into your personal bank and the SCPS teacher bank for others to use.

docs.title: Administering Assessments to Students
docs.text:
Once your assessment is created, you need to get it in front of students. MasteryConnect offers several ways to administer assessments — this guide covers the most common methods used at SCPS.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Method 1: Testing code (most common)
docs.text: A testing code is a unique code students enter to access the assessment on their devices.

docs.title: Generate a testing code
docs.text:
1. Open your Mastery Tracker and go to the Assessments tab.
  2. Find the assessment you want to administer.
  3. Click on the assessment, then click "Administer" or "Generate Code".
  4. A testing code will appear (a short alphanumeric code like A3X7K2).

docs.title: Share the code with students
docs.text:
1. Display the code on your projector, whiteboard, or share it verbally.
  2. Students go to masteryconnect.com and click "Enter Testing Code" (or they may have a student portal bookmark).
  3. They enter the code and begin the assessment.

Managing the testing window

  • You can generate a new code after class to prevent students from continuing the assessment outside of class time.
  • If a student gets disconnected, their progress is saved — they can re-enter the code to resume.

docs.title: Method 2: Through Canvas
docs.text:
If your tracker is linked to Canvas, you can push assessments directly into your Canvas course.

  1. When creating or administering the assessment, look for the option to "Push to Canvas" or "Create Canvas Assignment".
  2. The assessment will appear as an assignment in your Canvas course.
  3. Students access it just like any other Canvas assignment.

Why use Canvas delivery?

  • Students don't need a separate testing code
  • The assessment appears in their familiar Canvas workflow
  • Grades can sync back to Canvas (depending on your settings)

docs.title: Method 3: Paper-based with bubble sheets
docs.text:
For situations where devices aren't available or you prefer paper administration:

  1. In the assessment builder, make sure "Printable" is enabled.
  2. Print the assessment for students.
  3. Download bubble sheets from MasteryConnect — these come pre-slugged with student ID numbers.
  4. Students complete the bubble sheets by hand.
  5. See Scoring with bubble sheets for scanning and scoring instructions.

docs.title: What you can see
docs.text:
While students are testing, you can monitor progress from your tracker:

  • Which students have started the assessment
  • Which students have submitted the assessment
  • Real-time completion counts

docs.title: Managing student issues
docs.text: Issue Solution Student can't enter the code Verify the code is correct and the assessment is active Student got kicked out mid-test Their progress is saved — have them re-enter the code Student needs more time The assessment stays open as long as the code is active Student submitted by accident Contact your ITRT — submissions can sometimes be reopened Wrong student took the assessment Note it and contact your ITRT for data correction

docs.title: After the assessment
docs.text:
Once all students have submitted:

  1. Auto-scored items (multiple choice, true/false) will have results available immediately.
  2. Manually scored items (short answer, essay) will need to be scored in the Performance Grader.
  3. Navigate to your Mastery Tracker to see the updated data.

Performance Grader tip

When scoring in the Performance Grader, make sure to click "Save" for each student before clicking "Next". There is a known issue where the next arrow may not appear until you save first.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Scoring with bubble sheets — If you administered on paper
  • Reading the Mastery Tracker — Understand what the results mean
  • Running reports — Dig deeper into the data

docs.title: Scoring with Bubble Sheets
docs.text:
Bubble sheets let you administer paper-based assessments while still capturing data in MasteryConnect. This is useful when devices are limited or when you want a low-tech testing experience.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Before you begin
docs.text:
Prerequisites

  • Your assessment must have "Printable" enabled (set during assessment creation)
  • You need a printer and scanner (or a device camera with the MasteryConnect scanning feature)

docs.title: Step 1: Download and print bubble sheets
docs.text:
1. Open your Mastery Tracker and go to the Assessments tab.
  2. Find the assessment and click on it.
  3. Look for the "Bubble Sheets" or "Download Bubble Sheets" option.
  4. The bubble sheets will download as a PDF, pre-slugged with your students' ID numbers.
  5. Print one bubble sheet per student.

Pre-slugged sheets save time

Because each bubble sheet already has the student's ID, you don't need students to fill in their information. Just distribute the correct sheet to each student.

docs.title: Step 2: Administer the assessment
docs.text:
1. Print the assessment itself (separate from the bubble sheets).
  2. Distribute both the assessment and the matching bubble sheet to each student.
  3. Students answer questions on the bubble sheet, not on the assessment itself.
  4. Collect the bubble sheets when students are finished.

docs.title: Step 3: Scan the bubble sheets
docs.text:
MasteryConnect supports scanning through its web platform.

  1. Go to your assessment in MasteryConnect.
  2. Click "Score" or "Scan Bubble Sheets".
  3. Use your document scanner or device camera to scan each sheet.
  4. MasteryConnect will read the bubbles and auto-score the responses.

Scanning tips

  • Make sure sheets are flat and unwrinkled for best scanning accuracy.
  • Scan in good lighting if using a camera.
  • If a scan doesn't register correctly, you can manually enter scores as a backup.

docs.title: Step 4: Verify and finalize scores
docs.text:
1. After scanning, review the scores in MasteryConnect.
  2. Check for any flagged responses that the scanner couldn't read clearly.
  3. Manually correct any errors.
  4. Once everything looks good, the scores will automatically populate in your Mastery Tracker.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Reading the Mastery Tracker — See your results in the tracker
  • Running reports — Generate detailed reports from the data

docs.title: Creating an Assessment
docs.text:
Assessments in MasteryConnect are aligned to specific standards, giving you targeted data on what students know and can do. This guide covers creating an assessment from scratch and from the item bank.

Time to complete: ~10 minutes

docs.title: Before you begin
docs.text:
Prerequisites

  • Your Mastery Tracker is set up with standards (see Setting up your Mastery Tracker)
  • Your tracker is linked to Canvas (see Linking to Canvas)

docs.title: Option A: Create an assessment from the item bank
docs.text: This is the fastest way to build an assessment using pre-made, standards-aligned questions.

docs.title: Step 1: Open the assessment builder
docs.text:
1. From your Mastery Tracker, click the "Assessments" tab.
  2. Click "+ Create Assessment".
  3. Give your assessment a clear name (e.g., Unit 3 - Fractions Check).

docs.title: Step 2: Select your standard(s)
docs.text:
1. Choose the standard(s) this assessment will measure.
  2. You can assess one standard or multiple — but keep it focused for formative checks.

Keep it tight

For formative assessments, focus on 1–2 standards with 5–10 questions. This gives you actionable data without overwhelming students.

docs.title: Step 3: Browse the item bank
docs.text:
1. Click "Add Items from Bank" or "Browse Items".

  2. Filter items using these options:

    Filter Recommendation Standard Should auto-filter based on your selection Question Type Multiple choice and true/false are fastest to score DOK Level Mix DOK 1 (recall) and DOK 2 (application) for balanced data Difficulty Start with medium difficulty for a baseline

  3. Preview each item before adding it — click the item to see the full question.

  4. Check the box next to items you want, then click "Add Selected".

ELA teachers: a note about passages

If you're selecting items with reading passages, be intentional. Each passage adds reading time. A 5-question assessment with 5 different passages will take much longer than 5 questions on a single passage.

docs.title: Step 4: Review and finalize
docs.text:
1. Review the assessment in the builder. You can reorder questions by dragging them.
  2. Check that the answer key is correct for each item.
  3. Click "Create Assessment" to save it to your tracker.

docs.title: Option B: Create an assessment from scratch
docs.text: If you want to write your own questions or upload a PDF, use this method.

docs.title: Step 1: Start a new assessment
docs.text:
1. From the Assessments tab, click "+ Create Assessment".
  2. Name the assessment and select the standard(s).
  3. Choose "Author Items" instead of browsing the bank.

docs.title: Step 2: Add questions
docs.text:
1. Click "+ Add Question".

  2. Select the question type:

    • Multiple Choice — most common, auto-scored
    • True/False — quick checks
    • Short Answer — requires manual scoring
    • Performance Task — rubric-based, for deeper assessment

  3. Type your question and answer choices.

  4. Mark the correct answer by clicking on it.
  5. Align the question to a standard if it isn't already.

docs.title: Step 3: Upload a PDF (alternative)
docs.text:
If you have an existing assessment as a document:

  1. Choose "Upload PDF" in the assessment builder.
  2. Upload your file.
  3. Set the number and type of questions.
  4. Enter the answer key manually.

Don't include the answer key in your PDF

If you upload a PDF, make sure the answer key is not part of the document. Students will see the PDF directly.

docs.title: Step 4: Finalize
docs.text:
1. Review all questions and the answer key.
  2. Choose whether to make the assessment printable (enables bubble sheet scoring).
  3. Click "Create Assessment".

docs.title: After creating your assessment
docs.text:
Your assessment is now saved to your tracker but is in draft mode until you administer it.

Draft vs. Created

  • Draft: Only you can see it. You can still edit questions and answers.
  • Created: Locked in. Students can access it. You can still view reports after scoring.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Tips from your ITRT

  • Name assessments consistently: Unit # - Topic - Type (e.g., Unit 4 - Photosynthesis - Formative)
  • Limit standards per assessment: 1–2 for formative, 3–5 for summative
  • Preview as a student: Always preview your assessment before administering
  • Share with your team: Items you author go into the SCPS teacher bank — your colleagues can benefit from your work
  • Check question types: Only multiple choice and true/false are auto-scored. Short answer and performance tasks require manual grading.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Using the item bank — Deep dive into finding the best pre-built questions
  • Administering assessments — Get the assessment in front of students
  • Scoring with bubble sheets — For paper-based administration

docs.title: Using Data to Group Students
docs.text:
The ultimate goal of MasteryConnect isn't just to collect data — it's to act on it. This guide shows you how to use your tracker and report data to form targeted instructional groups.

Time to complete: ~10 minutes

docs.title: Why group by mastery level?
docs.text: Traditional grouping (high, medium, low) treats students as fixed categories. Mastery-based grouping is standard-specific — a student might be in the enrichment group for one standard and the intervention group for another. This is more targeted and more respectful of what students actually know.

docs.title: Step 1: Identify the standard to target
docs.text:
1. Open your Mastery Tracker.
  2. Look for a standard with a mix of colors — some green, some yellow, some red.
  3. This is your best candidate for small-group instruction because students have different needs.

If a whole column is red...

That's a whole-class reteach, not a small-group situation. Regroup after the reteach and re-assess.

docs.title: Step 2: Sort students by mastery level
docs.text: From your tracker, identify three groups based on the color coding for your target standard:

docs.title: Remediation group (red)
docs.text:
These students did not demonstrate mastery and need direct reteaching of the concept.

Instructional strategies:

  • Reteach using a different approach than the first time
  • Use manipulatives, visuals, or worked examples
  • Keep the group small (3–5 students if possible)
  • Reassess after the intervention

docs.title: Near mastery group (yellow)
docs.text:
These students are close but have specific gaps. They need targeted practice rather than full reteaching.

Instructional strategies:

  • Focus on the specific misconception (use your item analysis to identify it)
  • Provide guided practice with immediate feedback
  • Peer tutoring can work well for this group
  • A single focused session may be enough to move them to green

docs.title: Mastery group (green)
docs.text:
These students have demonstrated proficiency. They need enrichment or extension — not more of the same.

Instructional strategies:

  • Extension activities that deepen understanding
  • Apply the standard to a new or more complex context
  • Student-led projects or peer tutoring roles
  • Independent work while you focus on the other groups

docs.title: Step 3: Plan your grouping session
docs.text:
Here's a practical structure for a class period using mastery-based groups:

Time Remediation Group Near Mastery Group Mastery Group First 10 min Direct instruction with teacher Guided practice (task cards or partner work) Independent extension activity Next 10 min Guided practice with teacher Peer discussion and self-check Choice activity or peer tutoring Last 5 min Quick exit ticket (3–5 questions) Quick exit ticket Share or present extension work

You don't have to do this every day

Mastery-based grouping is a strategy to use when the data shows a clear split. It's not meant to replace your regular instruction flow — it's a tool for the right moment.

docs.title: Step 4: Reassess and regroup
docs.text:
After your intervention:

  1. Give a short reassessment (3–5 questions) on the same standard.
  2. Administer it through MasteryConnect so the data flows back into your tracker.
  3. Check the tracker — did students move from red/yellow to green?
  4. Regroup as needed — students who are now green move on. Students still in red may need a different approach.

docs.title: Using this in PLC conversations
docs.text:
Mastery-based grouping is even more powerful when done across classrooms:

  1. Compare tracker data with your team.
  2. Identify which teachers have high mastery rates on specific standards.
  3. Consider cross-classroom grouping: students rotate to the teacher whose instruction was most effective on that standard.
  4. Reassess together and celebrate growth.

This is what data-driven PLCs look like

When your PLC conversation starts with tracker data and ends with a grouping plan, you're doing exactly what MasteryConnect was designed to support.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Running reports — Get the detailed data you need for grouping decisions
  • Creating an assessment — Build a quick reassessment for your groups
  • Reading the Mastery Tracker — Monitor progress after regrouping

docs.title: Using the Item Bank
docs.text:
The MasteryConnect item bank is a library of pre-built, standards-aligned assessment questions. It includes questions created by MasteryConnect, shared by other educators, and items authored by SCPS teachers. Learning to navigate the bank efficiently will save you hours of assessment creation time.

Time to complete: ~8 minutes

docs.title: Accessing the item bank
docs.text:
There are two ways to get to the item bank:

From an assessmentFrom the main menu

While creating or editing an assessment, click "Add Items from Bank". This filters the bank to standards already selected for your assessment.

Click "Item Bank" in the left sidebar to browse all available items across all standards.

docs.title: Understanding item sources
docs.text:
Items in the bank come from different sources, and it helps to know which is which:

Source What It Means Mastery View Created by MasteryConnect/Instructure. Generally high quality and well-aligned. SCPS Teacher Bank Created by teachers in Shenandoah County. Relevant to our pacing and curriculum. Personal Bank Items you've authored yourself. Only visible to you unless shared. Community Shared by educators nationwide. Quality varies — always preview before using.

Start with Mastery View and SCPS

For the most reliable, well-aligned items, filter by Mastery View items first, then check the SCPS Teacher Bank. Community items can be useful but always preview them carefully.

docs.title: Filtering items effectively
docs.text: The power of the item bank is in the filters. Here's how to use them:

docs.title: Standard
docs.text:
• Select the specific Virginia SOL standard you're assessing.
  • Be as specific as possible — choosing a broad strand will return hundreds of results.

docs.title: Question type
docs.text: Type Best For Multiple Choice Quick formative checks, auto-scored True/False Quick recall checks, auto-scored Multi-Select Deeper understanding (select all that apply), auto-scored Short Answer Checking student reasoning, requires manual scoring Essay/Extended Response Performance-based assessment, requires manual scoring

docs.title: Difficulty and DOK level
docs.text:
• DOK 1 (Recall): Can the student remember the fact or definition?
  • DOK 2 (Skill/Concept): Can the student apply the concept?
  • DOK 3 (Strategic Thinking): Can the student reason and plan?

Mix your DOK levels

A good formative assessment includes a mix — a few DOK 1 items to check baseline recall, and DOK 2 items to see if students can apply what they've learned.

docs.title: Language
docs.text: Make sure English is selected unless you're intentionally looking for translated items.

docs.title: Previewing and selecting items
docs.text:
1. Click on any item to preview the full question, answer choices, and correct answer.
  2. Check that the item is:
    • Accurately aligned to the standard you're assessing
    • Clearly worded and free of errors
    • Appropriate for your students' reading level
  3. If the item looks good, check the box to select it.
  4. When you've selected all the items you want, click "Add Selected" to add them to your assessment.

docs.title: Authoring your own items
docs.text:
If you can't find what you need in the bank, you can create your own items that will be saved to your personal bank and the SCPS teacher bank.

  1. From the item bank or assessment builder, click "+ Create Item".
  2. Choose the question type.
  3. Write the question stem and answer choices.
  4. Mark the correct answer.
  5. Align it to a standard.
  6. Click "Save".

Help your colleagues

Every item you author goes into the SCPS teacher bank. Write clear, well-aligned questions and your teammates can use them too. Consider collaborating with your PLC to build a shared bank for your course.

docs.title: Common pitfalls
docs.text:
Watch out for these

  • Not previewing items: Some community items have errors or poor alignment. Always preview.
  • Too many passage-based items in ELA: Five questions with five different passages creates an unintentionally long assessment. Pick 1–2 passages and pull multiple questions from each.
  • Ignoring DOK levels: An assessment with all DOK 1 items won't tell you much about student understanding.
  • Forgetting to check the answer key: Occasionally items in the community bank have incorrect answer keys. Verify before administering.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Creating an assessment — Put your selected items into a complete assessment
  • Administering assessments — Get the assessment to your students

docs.title: Linking MasteryConnect to Canvas
docs.text:
Connecting your Mastery Tracker to your Canvas course is required at SCPS. This link ensures students can access assessments seamlessly and that reporting data stays consistent across both platforms.

Time to complete: ~5 minutes

docs.title: Before you begin
docs.text:
Prerequisites

  • Your Mastery Tracker must already be created (see Setting up your Mastery Tracker)
  • Your Canvas course must be published and have students enrolled
  • You need to be logged in to both MasteryConnect and Canvas

docs.title: Step 1: Open your Mastery Tracker
docs.text:
1. Log in to MasteryConnect.
  2. Navigate to Trackers in the left sidebar.
  3. Click on the tracker you want to link.

docs.title: Step 2: Access the link settings
docs.text:
1. Inside your tracker, click the gear icon () or "Settings" in the top right.
  2. Select "Link to Canvas" or "SIS Integration" from the menu.

docs.title: Step 3: Select your Canvas course
docs.text:
1. A list of your available Canvas courses will appear.
  2. Find and select the matching course (e.g., your Period 3 US History course).
  3. Click "Link" to connect them.

Match carefully

Make sure you're linking the correct period/section. The tracker name and Canvas course name should match. If you used the naming convention from the tracker setup guide, this should be straightforward.

docs.title: Step 4: Verify the student sync
docs.text:
After linking, your Canvas roster should sync to MasteryConnect automatically.

  1. Go back to your tracker and click "Students".
  2. Verify that your student list matches your Canvas roster.
  3. If students are missing, check that they are actively enrolled in your Canvas course.

Sync timing

The student sync may take a few minutes. If students don't appear right away, wait 5–10 minutes and refresh the page.

docs.title: Step 5: Test student access
docs.text:
Once linked, students can access MasteryConnect assessments directly through Canvas.

  1. In your Canvas course, students will see MasteryConnect assessments as assignments (if you push them to Canvas) or can access them via a testing code.
  2. Have a student verify they can see the assessment from their end.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
My Canvas course isn't showing up in the list
  • Make sure your Canvas course is published (not in draft mode)
  • Verify you're logged into MasteryConnect with the same account linked to your Canvas
  • Try refreshing the page or logging out and back in
Students are missing from my tracker after linking
  • Check that the students are actively enrolled in the Canvas course (not in a "pending" or "inactive" state)
  • Wait 5–10 minutes for the sync to complete
  • If the issue persists, contact your building ITRT
I linked to the wrong Canvas course
  • Go to tracker Settings → Link to Canvas
  • Unlink the current course
  • Select the correct course and relink

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Create an assessment — Now that your tracker is linked, build your first assessment
  • Administer an assessment — Learn how to push assessments to students through Canvas

docs.title: Reading the Mastery Tracker
docs.text:
The Mastery Tracker is a color-coded grid that shows you, at a glance, where every student stands on every standard. Understanding how to read it is essential for using MasteryConnect effectively.

Time to complete: ~8 minutes

docs.title: The tracker layout
docs.text:
The Mastery Tracker is organized as a grid:

  • Rows = Students (listed down the left side)
  • Columns = Standards (listed across the top)
  • Cells = Each cell represents one student's mastery level on one standard

docs.title: Understanding the colors
docs.text:
Each cell in the tracker is color-coded based on the student's performance:

Color Meaning What It Tells You Green Mastery Student has demonstrated proficiency on this standard Yellow Near Mastery Student is approaching proficiency but not there yet Red Remediation Student has not demonstrated proficiency and needs intervention Gray/Empty No Data No assessment has been scored for this standard yet

How are colors determined?

The color is based on the cut scores set for your tracker. By default:

  • Green (Mastery): 80%+ correct
  • Yellow (Near Mastery): 60–79% correct
  • Red (Remediation): Below 60% correct

These thresholds may be adjusted by your school or district. Check with your ITRT if you're unsure of your current settings.

docs.title: Reading patterns in your data
docs.text: The real power of the tracker is spotting patterns across students and standards.

docs.title: Pattern 1: A whole column is red
docs.text: If an entire standard column is red or mostly red, it means most of your class struggled with that standard. This is an instructional signal — you likely need to reteach this concept to the whole group.

docs.title: Pattern 2: A whole row is red
docs.text: If one student's entire row is red, that student is struggling broadly and may need intensive intervention beyond just one standard.

docs.title: Pattern 3: Mixed colors across a standard
docs.text: If a standard column shows a mix of green, yellow, and red, that's a signal for differentiated instruction — some students are ready to move on while others need support.

docs.title: Pattern 4: Green everywhere except one standard
docs.text: If a student is green on everything except one standard, that's a targeted intervention opportunity. A quick reteach or small group session on that one standard could move them to mastery.

docs.title: Tracker views
docs.text:
MasteryConnect offers different ways to view your tracker data:

Standards View (Default)Assessment List ViewStudent View

The standard grid view with students as rows and standards as columns. Best for day-to-day monitoring.

Shows assessments grouped by standard. You can see which assessments are in draft or created status, how many students have completed each one, and access reports directly. Toggle to this with the "Assessments" tab.

Click on any student's name to see their individual mastery profile across all standards. Great for parent conferences and student goal-setting conversations.

docs.title: What the tracker shows (and doesn't show)
docs.text:
Important to know

  • The tracker displays results from the most recent assessment for each standard by default. It does not average all assessments together.
  • If you've given multiple assessments on the same standard, the most recent one determines the color.
  • To see historical performance, use the reports feature (see Running reports).

docs.title: Using the tracker in PLCs and team meetings
docs.text:
The Mastery Tracker is a powerful tool for collaborative conversations:

  1. Identify common gaps: Which standards are red across multiple teachers' trackers?
  2. Share strategies: If one teacher's tracker is green on a standard others are struggling with, that teacher may have effective strategies to share.
  3. Plan interventions: Use the data to form small groups across classrooms.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Running reports — Go deeper with item analysis and student detail reports
  • Using data to group students — Turn tracker data into action

docs.title: Running Reports
docs.text:
MasteryConnect offers several report types that help you move from raw scores to instructional decisions. This guide covers the most useful reports for classroom teachers.

Time to complete: ~8 minutes

docs.title: Accessing reports
docs.text:
There are two ways to get to reports:

  1. From the tracker: Click the reporting icon () next to any assessment in the Assessments tab.
  2. From the main menu: Click "Reports" in the left sidebar for broader report options.

docs.title: Item analysis
docs.text:
What it shows: How each question performed — which questions students got right, which they missed, and the most common wrong answers.

Why it matters: This tells you not just that students struggled, but where exactly the misunderstanding is.

How to use it:

  1. Open the assessment in your tracker.
  2. Click the reporting icon or select "Item Analysis".
  3. Review each question:
    • High percentage correct (80%+): Students understood this concept.
    • Low percentage correct (below 60%): This needs reteaching.
    • Common wrong answer: Look at which distractor students picked most. This reveals the specific misconception.

The wrong answer is the goldmine

Don't just look at the percentage correct. Look at which wrong answer was most popular. That tells you exactly what students misunderstood, and you can target your reteach to that specific misconception.

docs.title: Student mastery report
docs.text:
What it shows: An individual student's performance across all assessed standards.

Why it matters: Perfect for parent conferences, IEP meetings, and student goal-setting conversations.

How to use it:

  1. Click on a student's name in your tracker.
  2. Select "Student Report" or "Mastery Report".
  3. You'll see a breakdown of every standard with their mastery level.
  4. You can print or export this report to share with parents or the student.

One-click sharing

MasteryConnect lets you generate individual student reports with a single click — handy when you need reports for an entire class before conferences.

docs.title: Standards mastery report
docs.text:
What it shows: How the entire class performed on each standard — aggregated data across all assessments.

Why it matters: Helps you see the big picture of where your class stands across the curriculum.

How to use it:

  1. From your tracker or the Reports menu, select "Standards Report".
  2. Review the percentage of students at mastery, near mastery, and remediation for each standard.
  3. Sort by lowest mastery to identify your biggest instructional priorities.

docs.title: Assessment comparison
docs.text:
What it shows: How the same students performed across multiple assessments on the same standard over time.

Why it matters: Shows growth. Did reteaching work? Are students progressing?

How to use it:

  1. From the Reports menu, select the assessment comparison option.
  2. Choose two or more assessments on the same standard.
  3. Compare the results side by side.

docs.title: Exporting and sharing reports
docs.text:
Most reports can be exported:

  • PDF: Best for printing or emailing to parents
  • CSV: Best for additional analysis in a spreadsheet

Look for the export icon () or "Download" button on any report screen.

docs.title: Best practices for reports
docs.text:
Tips from your ITRT

  • Run item analysis after every assessment — even if the overall scores look good. The question-level data reveals what you'd otherwise miss.
  • Use student reports for conferences — showing parents a color-coded standards map is far more meaningful than a single grade.
  • Bring standards reports to PLCs — share your data with your team and compare across classrooms.
  • Don't wait for summative data — run reports on formative assessments to adjust instruction in real time.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Using data to group students — Turn your report data into targeted instruction
  • Reading the Mastery Tracker — Go back to the tracker with fresh eyes

docs.title: Setting up your Mastery Tracker
docs.text:
The Mastery Tracker is the core of MasteryConnect — it's where you'll see student progress on each standard at a glance. This guide walks you through creating your first tracker.

Time to complete: ~10 minutes

docs.title: Before you begin
docs.text:
Prerequisites

  • You must have an active MasteryConnect account (provided by SCPS)
  • You need to know which course and standards you'll be tracking
  • Your Canvas course should already be set up (see Linking to Canvas for the next step)

docs.title: Step 1: Log in to MasteryConnect
docs.text:
1. Go to masteryconnect.com or access it through your Canvas dashboard via the MasteryConnect link.
  2. Sign in with your SCPS credentials. If this is your first time, you may need to use the SSO (Single Sign-On) option tied to your SCPS Google account.

Bookmark it

Save the MasteryConnect login page to your bookmarks bar for quick access.

docs.title: Step 2: Create a new tracker
docs.text:
1. From your MasteryConnect dashboard, click "Trackers" in the left sidebar.
  2. Click the "+ New Tracker" button (top right).

  3. You'll be prompted to set up your tracker with the following information:

    Field What to Enter Tracker Name Use a clear naming convention, e.g., Smith - US History - Period 3 Subject Select your subject area Grade Level Select the appropriate grade School Year Make sure the current school year is selected

Naming convention

Using a consistent format like LastName - Subject - Period makes it much easier for you and your administrators to find trackers later.

docs.title: Step 3: Add standards to your tracker
docs.text:
This is the most important step — you're telling MasteryConnect which standards you want to track for this class.

  1. After creating the tracker, click "Add Standards".
  2. Select Virginia Standards of Learning (SOL) as the standards set.
  3. Browse or search for the specific standards for your course.
  4. Check the box next to each standard you want to track.
  5. Click "Add Selected" to attach them to your tracker.

How many standards?

You don't need to add every SOL. Focus on the power standards or the standards your team has identified as priorities for the grading period. You can always add more later.

docs.title: Step 4: Organize your standards (optional but recommended)
docs.text:
Once your standards are added, you can drag and drop them to reorder them in a way that matches your pacing guide or curriculum map.

  1. Click on a standard and drag it to reorder.
  2. Standards at the top of the list will appear first in your tracker view.
  3. Consider grouping them by unit or reporting period for easier navigation.

docs.title: Step 5: Add students
docs.text:
Students can be added to your tracker in two ways:

Through Canvas (Recommended)Manually

If you link your tracker to Canvas, students will sync automatically. This is the preferred method at SCPS. See Linking MasteryConnect to Canvas.

  1. Open your tracker and click "Students"
  2. Click "+ Add Students"
  3. Search for students by name or ID number
  4. Select and add them to your tracker

Always link through Canvas

At SCPS, trackers must be linked through Canvas to ensure student access and accurate reporting. Manual entry should only be used as a temporary fix.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
Your tracker is set up! Here's what to do next:

  • Link your tracker to Canvas — Required for student access
  • Create your first assessment — Start collecting mastery data
  • Read the tracker display — Learn what the colors mean

Need help?

If your standards set doesn't include the Virginia SOLs you need, or if you're having trouble finding your students, contact your building ITRT.

docs.title: PowerTeacher Pro
docs.text: PowerTeacher Pro is the gradebook built into PowerSchool SIS. It's where your official grades live — the grades that go on report cards, transcripts, and the parent/student portal. Whether you grade directly in PowerTeacher Pro or sync grades from Canvas or Google Classroom, understanding this tool is essential.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Guide Time Description Navigating PowerTeacher Pro ~5 min Access the gradebook and understand the interface Setting up categories and calculations ~10 min Configure grade categories, weights, and calculation settings

docs.title: Assignments & Grading
docs.text: Guide Time Description Creating assignments and entering grades ~8 min Create assignments, enter scores, and use the Score Inspector Using the scoresheet ~5 min The traditional gradebook view for fast grade entry

docs.title: Grade Sync
docs.text: Guide Time Description Syncing grades from Canvas (GradeSync) ~10 min Set up and troubleshoot Canvas-to-PowerSchool grade passback Syncing grades from Google Classroom ~8 min Export grades from Classroom to PowerTeacher Pro

docs.title: Reports & End of Term
docs.text:
Guide Time Description Verifying grades and end-of-term tasks ~5 min Final grade verification, comments, and report card prep

SCPS-Specific Notes

  • PowerTeacher Pro is your official gradebook of record. Grades entered here appear on report cards, transcripts, and the PowerSchool parent/student portal.
  • Use Chrome for the best PowerTeacher Pro experience. It's fastest and most reliable in Chrome.
  • Grade categories may be pre-set by the district. Do not create your own categories if district categories have been provided — grades using custom categories may not calculate into the final grade correctly.
  • If you grade in Canvas or Google Classroom, you must sync those grades to PowerTeacher Pro for them to count on report cards.

docs.title: Creating Assignments and Entering Grades
docs.text:
Assignments are the graded items that live inside your categories. This guide covers creating assignments, entering scores, and using the Score Inspector.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: Creating an assignment
docs.text:
1. Click "+ Create" (top right) → "Assignment".
  2. Fill in the details:
Field What to Enter Name Descriptive name (keep it under 30 characters if you use Canvas GradeSync) Category Select the appropriate district category Score Type Points, Percentage, or Letter Grade Points Possible Maximum points for the assignment Due Date When the assignment is due Include in Final Grade Check this unless it's a practice/ungraded assignment
  1. Click Save.

docs.title: Publishing options
docs.text:
On the Publish tab, choose when the assignment appears on the parent/student portal:

  • Days before due date — Shows the assignment a set number of days early
  • On due date — Only visible on the due date
  • Never — Hidden from the portal (useful for internal tracking like benchmark assessments)

docs.title: From the Assignments page
docs.text:
1. Click Grading → Assignments.
  2. Click on an assignment name.
  3. Student names appear with score entry fields.
  4. Click in the score field next to a student's name and type the grade.
  5. Click Save.

docs.title: Using the Score Inspector
docs.text:
When you click in a score cell, the Score Inspector panel appears on the right:

  • Enter the numeric score using the keypad
  • Add score flags: Late, Missing, Absent, Exempt, Collected, Incomplete
  • Add a comment about the student's score
  • Apply special codes if your school uses them (NC for no credit, etc.)

docs.title: Quick Fill
docs.text:
Need to give the whole class the same score?

  1. Enter the score for one student.
  2. Click the Fill icon (down arrow) in the Score Inspector.
  3. Choose to fill all empty fields with that score.

Mark exceptions first, then Quick Fill

Mark any students who are absent, exempt, or missing first. Then use Quick Fill to enter the default score for everyone else. This prevents accidentally overwriting special statuses.

docs.title: Score flags explained
docs.text: Flag What It Means How It Affects the Grade Late Submitted past the due date Score still counts (unless your school has a late policy) Missing Not submitted Counts as a zero unless changed Absent Student was absent Does not count in the grade calculation Exempt Excused from the assignment Does not count in the grade calculation Collected Received but not yet graded No impact until a score is entered Incomplete Started but not finished Depends on school policy

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Using the scoresheet — Enter grades faster in the grid view
  • Setting up categories and calculations — Make sure your categories are correct

docs.title: Setting Up Categories and Calculations
docs.text:
Grade categories and calculation settings determine how individual assignment scores roll up into quarter, semester, and final grades. Getting this right is critical.

Time to complete: ~10 min

docs.title: Understanding categories
docs.text:
Categories group similar assignments together (e.g., Classwork, Homework, Assessments, Projects). Each category can be weighted differently in the final grade calculation.

Use district categories

SCPS may provide pre-set district categories. If so, do not create your own — grades assigned to custom categories that aren't recognized by the district calculation setup won't factor into final grades. Check with your ITRT or building admin before making changes.

docs.title: Viewing and adjusting your categories
docs.text:
1. Click Grading → Categories.
  2. You'll see a list of available categories.
  3. To edit a category, click the pencil icon next to it.
  4. To hide categories you don't use, click the pencil and set it to Inactive. This cleans up your dropdown list when creating assignments.

docs.title: Setting category defaults
docs.text:
For each category you use, you can set defaults that auto-apply when you create new assignments:

  1. Click the pencil icon on a category.
  2. Set default score type (points, percentage, letter grade).
  3. Set default points possible.
  4. Set publishing options for the parent/student portal.
  5. Click Save.

docs.title: Setting up Traditional Grade Calculations
docs.text:
This is where you tell PowerTeacher Pro how to weight your categories.

  1. Click your name (bottom left) → Settings.
  2. Select "Traditional Grade Calculations".
  3. You'll see folders for each reporting term (Q1, Q2, S1, F1, etc.) — these are set by the district.
  4. Click the pencil icon under Actions for a term.
  5. Choose "Category Weighting" as the calculation type.
  6. Set the percentage weight for each category.
Category Example Weight Classwork 30% Homework 20% Assessments 40% Projects 10%

Weights must match your school's grading policy

Check with your building administration for the correct weighting scheme. This may be standardized across your school or grade level.

docs.title: Copying calculations to other terms and classes
docs.text: Once you've set up one quarter correctly, you don't need to redo it:

docs.title: Copy within a class (Q1 to Q2, Q3, Q4)
docs.text:
1. In the Traditional Grade Calculations screen, click the gear icon (top right).
  2. Select "Within a Class".
  3. Choose the source term and destination term(s).
  4. Check "Recalculate final grades".
  5. Click Validate, then Copy.

docs.title: Copy to other classes
docs.text:
1. Same gear icon → select "To Other Classes".
  2. Choose the source class and destination class(es).
  3. Validate and Copy.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Creating assignments and entering grades — Start adding assignments to your categories
  • Using the scoresheet — Enter grades in the traditional grid view

docs.title: Verifying Grades and End-of-Term Tasks
docs.text:
Before report cards go out, take time to verify your grades, add comments, and make sure everything in PowerTeacher Pro is accurate.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: 1. Verify all assignments are graded
docs.text:
1. Open the Scoresheet.
  2. Look for any empty cells — these are missing grades.
  3. Decide whether each should be a zero, exempt, absent, or missing flag.
  4. Enter appropriate scores or flags for all remaining blanks.

Blanks are not zeros

In PowerTeacher Pro, a blank cell is excluded from the grade calculation — it doesn't count as a zero. If a student didn't submit an assignment and should receive a zero, you must enter 0 explicitly.

docs.title: 2. Check category totals and overall grades
docs.text:
1. On the Scoresheet, review the category total and overall grade columns.
  2. Spot-check a few students to make sure the calculated grade matches what you expect.
  3. If something looks off, check your category weights in Settings → Traditional Grade Calculations.

docs.title: 3. Add report card comments
docs.text:
1. Go to Grading → Reporting Terms.
  2. Select the current term.
  3. For each student, click in the comment field.
  4. Type your comment or use the Comment Bank to select pre-written comments.
  5. Click Save.

Comment Bank

PowerTeacher Pro has a Comment Bank where you can save frequently used comments. Access it from the comment field. You can also import comments from previous years if you used the old PowerTeacher Gradebook.

docs.title: 4. Verify GradeSync (if using Canvas or Google Classroom)
docs.text:
If you sync grades from another platform:

  1. Run a final manual sync from Canvas or export from Google Classroom.
  2. Open PowerTeacher Pro and verify that the latest grades are reflected.
  3. Check for any sync errors on the Grade Sync status page in Canvas.

docs.title: 5. Confirm grades are final
docs.text:
Once you've verified everything, your grades are ready for report cards. Your building admin will communicate the deadline for finalizing grades and when the reporting term will be locked.

Once locked, you can't edit

After the reporting term is locked by administration, you cannot change grades. Make all corrections before the deadline.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Using the scoresheet — Review grades one more time
  • Syncing grades from Canvas — Run a final sync before locking

docs.title: Syncing Grades from Canvas (GradeSync)
docs.text:
If you grade assignments in Canvas, GradeSync lets you push those grades to PowerTeacher Pro so they appear on report cards and the parent portal. This guide covers setup, syncing, and troubleshooting.

Time to complete: ~10 min

docs.title: Before you begin
docs.text:
Prerequisites

  • Your Canvas course must be connected to PowerSchool (typically set up by your district)
  • Your Canvas assignment groups must match your PowerTeacher Pro categories
  • You need to have opened PowerTeacher Pro at least once for each course to "enable" the categories

docs.title: Step 1: Match your categories
docs.text:
Your Canvas assignment groups need to align with your PowerTeacher Pro categories.

  1. In Canvas, go to your course → Assignments page.
  2. Click the three dots (vertical ellipse) next to the blue "+ Assignment" button.
  3. Select "Sync SIS Categories".
  4. For each Canvas assignment group, use the dropdown to select the matching PowerTeacher Pro category.
  5. Save your selections.

Keep names consistent

If your Canvas group is called "Classwork" and your PowerTeacher category is called "Classwork," matching is intuitive. If they have different names, you can still map them — just make sure each Canvas group points to the right PowerTeacher category.

docs.title: Step 2: Enable assignments for sync
docs.text:
Each individual assignment must be marked for syncing:

  1. Open an assignment in Canvas.
  2. In the assignment settings, look for the "Sync to SIS" or "Post to SIS" checkbox.
  3. Check it.
  4. Make sure the assignment has a due date — assignments without due dates won't sync.

Assignment name length

Assignment names must be 50 characters or fewer to sync successfully. Longer names will cause sync errors.

docs.title: Step 3: Sync grades
docs.text:
Manual syncAutomatic nightly sync
  1. In Canvas, go to Grades (Gradebook).
  2. Click the Actions menu (or three dots).
  3. Select "Sync to SIS".
  4. Review the assignments and grades being synced.
  5. Click Sync.
  1. In your Canvas course, go to Settings → Navigation.
  2. Enable "Grade Sync" (drag it to the active area).
  3. Click on Grade Sync in your course navigation.
  4. Go to the Settings tab.
  5. Toggle "Nightly Sync" on.
  6. Grades will sync automatically every evening.

docs.title: Troubleshooting common sync issues
docs.text:
Problem Solution Assignment didn't sync Check that the "Sync to SIS" box is checked and a due date is set Categories don't match Go to Assignments → three dots → "Sync SIS Categories" and re-map Assignment name too long Shorten the name to 50 characters or fewer No score entered Enter at least one student's score — empty assignments may not sync Grades synced incorrectly Remember: Canvas sync overwrites PowerTeacher grades. Make corrections in Canvas, not PowerTeacher. Sync button not visible Enable "Grade Sync" in your course Settings → Navigation

Canvas is the source of truth

Once you set up GradeSync, make grade corrections in Canvas, not PowerTeacher Pro. The next sync will overwrite any changes you make directly in PowerTeacher.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Verifying grades and end-of-term tasks — Check everything before report cards
  • Setting up categories and calculations — Make sure your PowerTeacher categories are correct

docs.title: Syncing Grades from Google Classroom
docs.text:
If you use Google Classroom for assignments, you can export grades to PowerTeacher Pro so they appear on report cards and the parent portal.

Time to complete: ~8 min

docs.title: How the integration works
docs.text:
Google Classroom integrates with PowerSchool SIS, allowing teachers to:

  • Export grades from Google Classroom to PowerTeacher Pro
  • Import rosters and grading categories from PowerSchool into Classroom

Admin setup required

The Google Classroom–PowerSchool connection must be set up by your SCPS admin. If you don't see the SIS export option in Google Classroom, check with your ITRT.

docs.title: Exporting grades from Google Classroom
docs.text:
1. Open your class in Google Classroom.
  2. Go to the Grades tab.
  3. Look for the SIS export option (this appears if the integration is enabled).
  4. Select the assignments you want to export.
  5. Map your Google Classroom grade categories to the corresponding PowerTeacher Pro categories.
  6. Click Export.

Grades will transfer to PowerTeacher Pro for the mapped assignments.

docs.title: Alternative: Manual CSV transfer
docs.text:
If the direct integration isn't enabled, you can transfer grades manually:

  1. In Google Classroom, go to the Grades tab.
  2. Click the gear icon → "Copy all grades to Google Sheets".
  3. Open the Google Sheet and download it as a CSV file.
  4. In PowerTeacher Pro, go to your course → click the gear icon → "Import Scores".
  5. Upload the CSV file and map the columns to match your students and assignments.

Check student matching

When importing, make sure student names or IDs in the CSV match what PowerTeacher Pro expects. Mismatches will cause grades to land on the wrong students or fail to import.

docs.title: Best practices
docs.text:
Tips from your ITRT

  • Pick one primary gradebook: If you're grading in Google Classroom, consistently export to PowerTeacher Pro. Don't split some grades in Classroom and others directly in PowerTeacher — it creates confusion.
  • Export regularly: Don't wait until the end of the quarter. Export weekly or after each major assignment to keep PowerTeacher current.
  • Verify after export: Always spot-check a few students in PowerTeacher Pro after exporting to make sure grades transferred correctly.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Verifying grades and end-of-term tasks — Final grade checks before report cards
  • Setting up categories and calculations — Ensure PowerTeacher categories match

docs.title: Navigating PowerTeacher Pro
docs.text:
PowerTeacher Pro is accessed through the PowerSchool portal. This guide walks you through getting in and understanding the main interface.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Accessing PowerTeacher Pro
docs.text:
1. Log in to the PowerSchool portal with your SCPS credentials.
  2. From the navigation menu, click "PowerTeacher Pro" — or click it from beneath your course listing.
  3. The gradebook opens showing your classes.

Use Chrome

PowerTeacher Pro runs fastest and most reliably in Google Chrome. If you're experiencing slowness or display issues, switch to Chrome.

docs.title: Class selector
docs.text: When you first open PowerTeacher Pro, you'll see your class list. Click on any class to open its gradebook. You can customize class names and colors for easy identification.

docs.title: Key menu areas
docs.text: Area What It Does Grading Access the Scoresheet, Assignments, Categories, and Standards pages Students View student demographics, schedules, and individual grade details Settings Configure Traditional Grade Calculations, Display Settings, and preferences Create Quick-create new assignments and categories

docs.title: The Quick Menu
docs.text: Look for arrows next to page headings — clicking these opens a Quick Menu with shortcuts to related areas. This saves a lot of clicking through menus.

docs.title: Switching between classes
docs.text: Use the class dropdown at the top of the page to switch between your classes without going back to the main selector.

docs.title: Key icons and indicators
docs.text: Icon Meaning Lock icon on an assignment column The reporting term or class is locked — you cannot edit grades Blue comment icon in a score cell A comment exists for that student's grade Flag icons (Late, Missing, Absent, etc.) Score indicators that mark submission status Green checkmark Collected/completed

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Setting up categories and calculations — Configure how grades are calculated
  • Creating assignments and entering grades — Start grading

docs.title: Using the Scoresheet
docs.text:
The Scoresheet is the traditional gradebook grid — students as rows, assignments as columns. It's the fastest way to enter multiple grades at once.

Time to complete: ~5 min

docs.title: Accessing the Scoresheet
docs.text:
1. Click Grading → Scoresheet.
  2. The grid view opens with all students and assignments.

docs.title: Entering grades in the Scoresheet
docs.text:
1. Click on any cell to select it.
  2. Type the score using your keyboard or the Score Inspector keypad.
  3. Press Tab to move to the next student (down) or Enter to save.
  4. Click Save when finished.

docs.title: Color coding
docs.text:
• Cells are color-coded by category — this helps you quickly identify which assignments belong to which category.
  • You can customize category colors in the Categories settings.

docs.title: Viewing totals and averages
docs.text:
The Scoresheet shows category totals and overall grade columns:

  • Category Total columns show the calculated grade for each category
  • Overall Grade appears at the far right based on your weighting setup
  • These update in real time as you enter scores

docs.title: Sorting and filtering
docs.text:
• Click a column header to sort by that assignment
  • Use the filter options (gear icon) to show/hide specific categories or date ranges
  • Toggle "Show Category Totals" on or off for a cleaner view

docs.title: Exporting the Scoresheet
docs.text:
1. Click the export icon (top right of the Scoresheet).
  2. Choose Excel or CSV format.
  3. Download your gradebook for backup or analysis.

docs.title: What's next?
docs.text:
• Syncing grades from Canvas — If you grade in Canvas, sync here
  • End-of-term tasks — Verify grades before report cards
